100.title: Vaginal Itching
100.diagnosis: Vulvovaginal Candidiasis (VVC) or other fungal infection, Bacterial Vaginosis (BV), Atrophic Vaginitis (in postmenopausal women), Contact dermatitis, or allergic reaction are possible causes of vaginal itching.
exam: Vaginal Itching — exam tailored to chief complaint on arrival.
Soft, non-distended, no focal peritoneal signs on initial exam.
Alert and oriented unless perfusion or metabolic derangement present.
No acute rash; capillary refill and perfusion assessed.
100.clinical_tip: Positive pregnancy test changes everything — ectopic until proven otherwise.
100.objective: Rule out ectopic, torsion, and rupture.

interventions.label: Send lab work including wet mount, culture and sensitivity for vaginal fluid, KOH prep for fungal elements, CBC
Prescribe an antifungal cream or oral medication (such as fluconazole) for empiric treatment of VVC
Consider sending a swab for possible allergy testing if history of new feminine hygiene product use is present
Order: Order: time-sensitive interventions first; (oxygen, IV access, glucose check)
interventions.why: vital signs and oxygen are important for any patient, but especially in a case of vaginal itching where the diagnosis is unknown

interventions.label: Pregnancy test (β-hCG)
interventions.why: it is essential to rule out pregnancy in women of childbearing age, as certain conditions can affect both mother and fetus

easy.clinicalTip: Teaching hint: Positive pregnancy test changes everything — ectopic until proven otherwise.
easy.objective: Rule out ectopic, torsion, and rupture. Start with stabilization, then targeted testing.

standard.clinicalTip: Positive pregnancy test changes everything — ectopic until proven otherwise.
standard.objective: Rule out ectopic, torsion, and rupture.

hard.clinicalTip: Minimal coaching — prioritize life threats without hand-holding.
hard.objective: High-acuity workup: Rule out ectopic, torsion, and rupture.

101.title: Fever
101.diagnosis: Undifferentiated Fever in an Adult or Pediatric Patient
exam: Fever — exam tailored to chief complaint on arrival.
Soft, non-distended, no focal peritoneal signs on initial exam.
Alert and oriented unless perfusion or metabolic derangement present.
Warm skin with fever; no purpura on initial survey.
101.clinical_tip: Petechiae + fever in a sick patient — treat empirically for meningococcemia.
101.objective: Recognize toxic rash syndromes. Resuscitate and cover infection.

interventions.label: Consider obtaining a lumbar puncture (in certain situations)
Oxygen
interventions.why: The patient is experiencing fever, which could be a symptom of various conditions including infections. Oxygen is necessary to support the patient's breathing.

interventions.label: IV access ×2
interventions.why: IV access is crucial for administering fluids and medications, especially in a critical situation like this.

interventions.label: universal labs + imaging
interventions.why: Universal labs such as CBC, CMP, UA, and imaging like a chest X-ray or CT scan are important for determining the cause of fever.

easy.clinicalTip: Teaching hint: Petechiae + fever in a sick patient — treat empirically for meningococcemia.
easy.objective: Recognize toxic rash syndromes. Resuscitate and cover infection. Start with stabilization, then targeted testing.

standard.clinicalTip: Petechiae + fever in a sick patient — treat empirically for meningococcemia.
standard.objective: Recognize toxic rash syndromes. Resuscitate and cover infection.

102.title: Generalized Weakness
102.diagnosis: Neurological Disorder (such as Guillain-Barre Syndrome, Myasthenia Gravis, or Multiple Sclerosis)
102.vitalsText: Initial Vital Signs Day 1 @ 9:00 Temperature: 37.0 C (98.6 degrees F) Pulse: 110 beat/min, Regular rhythm; bounding Respiratory rate: 18 /minute Blood pressure, systolic: 122 mm Hg Blood pressure, diastolic: 74 mm Hg Height: 175.3 cm (69.0 in) Weight: 65.8 kg (145 lb) Body Mass Index: 21.4 kg/m2
exam: presents to your office complaining of progressive generalized weakness that has been occurring for the past 3 months.
Soft, non-distended, no focal peritoneal signs on initial exam.
Motor strength and reflexes tested for fatigability and focal deficits.
Skin rash morphology and distribution documented.
102.clinical_tip: Weakness + hyperreflexia vs hyporeflexia localizes — think electrolytes and cord.
102.objective: Differentiate metabolic, neurologic, and toxic causes.

interventions.label: Laboratory tests: Complete Blood Count (CBC), Electrolytes, BUN, Creatinine, Glucose, LFTs, CMP, Coagulation Profile, Thyroid Function Tests (TFTs), Rheumatoid Factor, ANA, Vitamin B12 and Folate levels, CRP, ESR
Imaging studies: MRI with contrast of the brain and spine
Neurology consultation for further evaluation and management
Nerve conduction studies (if not performed already)
Respiratory support as needed due to potential risk of respiratory failure
Oxygen
interventions.why: The patient presents with generalized weakness, which could be a sign of hypoxia. Immediate oxygen administration is crucial.

interventions.label: Order: Order: time-sensitive interventions first; (oxygen, IV access, glucose check)
interventions.why: Establishing IV access and checking glucose levels are essential initial steps to address potential metabolic issues contributing to the patient's weakness.

easy.intro: Initial History Reason(s) for visit: Generalized weakness History of present Illness: A previously healthy 30-year-old female with a PMH of asthma presents to your office complaining of progressive generalized weakness that has been occurring for the past 3 months. Her symptoms worsen at the end of the day, and her weakness improves with rest. She reports occasional blurry vision with improvement after closing and resting her eyes. She also notes difficulty chewing food and swallowing recently. She has also been experiencing dyspnea with strenuous exercise, but it is now refractory to her albuterol inhaler. Past Medical History: She broke her arm when she was 14 which required an open reduction and pin placement. Current medications: Birth control, multi-vitamin Allergies: Penicillin (rash and shortness of breath) Vaccinations: Received Childhood vaccinations. Women's health: She contracted gonorrhea from a sexual partner at age 20 which was successfully treated. She had a LEEP procedure done due to an abnormal pap smear 6 years ago. Her last pap smear was done 3 years ago which was normal. Family History: Her father has a history of congestive heart failure and is status post two myocardial infarctions. Her mother has hypothyroidism. Her grandfather died of a stroke. Social History: Marital/Family: The patient is single. She is currently living and is sexually active with her boyfriend of 2 years. Personal habits: She states that she drinks occasionally. The patient smokes cigars with her boyfriend on occasion when going out. Occupation/educational: She works as a newspaper editor for a local newspaper. Recreational: She enjoys reading and breeds dogs. Review of systems: General: See HPI Skin: Negative HEENT: See HPI Cardiorespiratory: Negative Gastrointestinal: Negative Genitourinary: Negative Neuropsychiatric: See HPI

103.title: Routine Check-Up
exam: Routine Check-Up — exam tailored to chief complaint on arrival.
Soft, non-distended, no focal peritoneal signs on initial exam.
Alert and oriented unless perfusion or metabolic derangement present.
No acute rash; capillary refill and perfusion assessed.
103.clinical_tip: Stabilize first — ABCs, IV access, monitor, then targeted workup.
103.objective: Identify and treat life threats within the time limit.

interventions.label: Complete Blood Count (CBC)
interventions.why: Helps to evaluate general health status, detect infections and anemia.

interventions.label: Comprehensive Metabolic Panel (CMP)
interventions.why: Assesses the metabolism of carbohydrates, proteins, and fats, and helps to identify electrolyte imbalances, kidney function, and liver disease.

interventions.label: Urinalysis
interventions.why: Helps to evaluate kidney function, detect infections, or abnormalities such as protein or glucose in the urine.

interventions.label: Electrocardiogram (ECG)
interventions.why: Assesses the heart's electrical activity and can help identify potential cardiac problems.

interventions.label: Chest X-ray
interventions.why: Helps to diagnose conditions affecting the lungs, heart, and chest.

interventions.label: Consultation with Internal Medicine or Hospitalist
interventions.why: For further evaluation, management, and potential diagnostic procedures.

easy.objective: Identify and treat life threats within the time limit. Start with stabilization, then targeted testing.

104.title: Back Pain
104.diagnosis: Acute Low Back Pain of Undetermined Origin
exam: Back Pain — exam tailored to chief complaint on arrival.
Soft, non-distended, no focal peritoneal signs on initial exam.
Alert and oriented unless perfusion or metabolic derangement present.
No acute rash; capillary refill and perfusion assessed.
104.clinical_tip: Weakness + hyperreflexia vs hyporeflexia localizes — think electrolytes and cord.
104.objective: Differentiate metabolic, neurologic, and toxic causes.

interventions.label: Obtain a detailed history, including the location, severity, onset, and aggravating/relieving factors of the back pain.
Perform a comprehensive physical examination with focus on neurological and orthopedic assessments.
Order diagnostic imaging such as X-rays or MRI to evaluate for potential causes like herniated discs, fractures, or spinal stenosis.
Refer patient to a specialist (e.g., Orthopedist, Neurologist, Physical Therapist) if the diagnosis remains uncertain after initial evaluation and imaging.
Prescribe non-steroidal anti-inflammatory drugs (NSAIDs) for pain management as needed.
order1
interventions.why: The text is clearly legible and appears to be a standard clinical order.

interventions.label: Oxygen
interventions.why: Back pain can be indicative of serious conditions that may require oxygen therapy. IV access is necessary for administering medication and fluids.

interventions.why: The patient may need fluids or medications intravenously.

105.title: Abdominal Pain
105.vitalsText: Initial Vital Signs Day 1 @ 18:00 Temperature: 37.2 C (98.9 degrees F) Pulse: 106 beat/min, Tachycardic Respiratory rate: 20 /minute Blood pressure, systolic: 114 mm Hg Blood pressure, diastolic: 79 mm Hg Height: 160.0 cm (63.0 in) Weight: 80.5 kg (177.4 lb) Body Mass Index: 31.4 kg/m2
exam: presents to the emergency room with abdominal pain, lethargy, and vomiting x2 days.
Abdominal tenderness pattern matches history; no rigid abdomen documented yet.
Alert and oriented unless perfusion or metabolic derangement present.
No acute rash; capillary refill and perfusion assessed.
105.clinical_tip: Elderly and immunocompromised may have minimal exam findings until rupture.
105.objective: Resuscitate, image, involve surgery early if indicated.

interventions.label: Obtain vital signs and physical examination
interventions.why: To assess the patient's overall condition and identify any immediate life-threatening conditions

interventions.label: Laboratory tests: CBC, Metabolic Panel, Lipase
Imaging: Abdominal CT scan with contrast
Consults: General Surgery, Emergency Medicine (if not the initial provider)
Treatment: NPO (nil per os) status, IV fluid administration, pain management
Oxygen
interventions.why: The patient is experiencing abdominal pain, which could indicate a variety of serious conditions that require immediate oxygen therapy.

interventions.label: IV access ×2
interventions.why: The patient needs IV access for administration of fluids and potentially other medications.

interventions.label: glucose check
interventions.why: A glucose check is important to rule out hypoglycemia as a possible cause of the abdominal pain.

easy.intro: Initial History Reason(s) for visit: Abdominal Pain History of present Illness: A 22-year-old female presents to the emergency room with abdominal pain, lethargy, and vomiting x2 days. 106.title: Fatigue
106.diagnosis: Anemia (iron deficiency, vitamin B12 or folate deficiency anemia are common causes of fatigue in a patient without obvious symptoms)
exam: Fatigue — exam tailored to chief complaint on arrival.
Soft, non-distended, no focal peritoneal signs on initial exam.
Alert and oriented unless perfusion or metabolic derangement present.
No acute rash; capillary refill and perfusion assessed.
106.clinical_tip: Stabilize first — ABCs, IV access, monitor, then targeted workup.
106.objective: Identify and treat life threats within the time limit.

interventions.label: Order complete blood count (CBC) with differential and reticulocyte count
If CBC shows low hemoglobin, hematocrit or red blood cell count, consider ordering serum ferritin, total iron binding capacity, vitamin B12, and folate levels
Oxygen
interventions.why: The patient is experiencing fatigue, which could be a sign of hypoxia or respiratory distress. Oxygen should be administered immediately.

interventions.label: IV access ×2
interventions.why: Intravenous access is essential for providing fluids, medications, and blood products if needed.

interventions.label: glucose check
interventions.why: Hypoglycemia can cause symptoms similar to fatigue. A glucose check helps rule out this potentially life-threatening condition.

easy.clinicalTip: Teaching hint: Stabilize first — ABCs, IV access, monitor, then targeted workup.
easy.objective: Identify and treat life threats within the time limit. Start with stabilization, then targeted testing.

107.title: Paranoia
107.diagnosis: Psychiatric Emergency (e.g., Acute Psychosis, Schizophrenia Spectrum Disorder, Substance-Induced Psychosis)
exam: Paranoia — exam tailored to chief complaint on arrival.
Soft, non-distended, no focal peritoneal signs on initial exam.
Alert and oriented unless perfusion or metabolic derangement present.
No acute rash; capillary refill and perfusion assessed.
107.clinical_tip: Stabilize first — ABCs, IV access, monitor, then targeted workup.
107.objective: Identify and treat life threats within the time limit.

interventions.label: Obtain vital signs
interventions.why: To assess the general health status of the patient

interventions.label: Perform a thorough psychiatric evaluation including mental status examination
interventions.why: To evaluate the patient's mental state, cognitive function, and behavior

interventions.label: Obtain urine drug screen
interventions.why: To identify potential substance use that may be contributing to the patient's symptoms

interventions.label: Consult Psychiatry for further evaluation and management
interventions.why: For a thorough psychiatric assessment and treatment plan

interventions.label: Order electrolyte panel, complete blood count, glucose, thyroid function tests, and toxicology screen if indicated by clinical findings or substance use history
interventions.why: To rule out other medical causes that may be contributing to the patient's symptoms

interventions.label: If the patient is agitated or violent, administer haloperidol 5mg IM initially, followed by repeat doses as needed for control of symptoms (under close psychiatric monitoring)
interventions.why: To provide immediate management of acute agitation and aggression in a safe manner

interventions.label: Ensure patient safety: use soft restraints if necessary, observe continuously, and consider a brief hospitalization if needed
interventions.why: To ensure the safety of both the patient and staff during the initial evaluation and treatment process

interventions.label: IV access x2
interventions.why: For critical Principle in Step 3 CCS, if you delay stabilization while ordering labs, your score plummets — even with correct diagnosis.

108.title: Abdominal Pain
108.vitalsText: Initial Vital Signs Day 1 @ 18:00 Temperature: 37.2 C (98.9 degrees F) Pulse: 106 beat/min, Tachycardic Respiratory rate: 20 /minute Blood pressure, systolic: 114 mm Hg Blood pressure, diastolic: 79 mm Hg Height: 160.0 cm (63.0 in) Weight: 80.5 kg (177.4 lb) Body Mass Index: 31.4 kg/m2
exam: presents to the emergency room with abdominal pain, lethargy, and vomiting x2 days.
Abdominal tenderness pattern matches history; no rigid abdomen documented yet.
Alert and oriented unless perfusion or metabolic derangement present.
No acute rash; capillary refill and perfusion assessed.
108.clinical_tip: Elderly and immunocompromised may have minimal exam findings until rupture.
108.objective: Resuscitate, image, involve surgery early if indicated.

interventions.label: Immediate abdominal examination and resuscitation
interventions.why: To stabilize the patient and assess vital signs, level of consciousness, and physical findings.

interventions.label: Laboratory tests: Complete Blood Count (CBC), Chemistry panel, Lipase, Amylase
Imaging: Non-contrast CT Abdomen/Pelvis or Ultrasound Abdomen
Consults: General Surgery and Emergency Medicine
Further diagnostic workup based on imaging results
Oxygen
interventions.why: To provide adequate oxygenation for the patient experiencing abdominal pain.

interventions.label: IV access ×2
interventions.why: For fluid resuscitation and potential administration of medications.

interventions.label: Placement of patient in appropriate location (ER → ICU vs floor)
interventions.why: Based on the nature and severity of the abdominal pain.

109.title: Unresponsive
109.diagnosis: Possible causes could include acute cerebrovascular event, sepsis, drug overdose, metabolic or electrolyte imbalance, or head trauma
exam: Unresponsive — exam tailored to chief complaint on arrival.
Soft, non-distended, no focal peritoneal signs on initial exam.
Alert and oriented unless perfusion or metabolic derangement present.
Warm skin with fever; no purpura on initial survey.
109.clinical_tip: Fingerstick glucose before CT — hypoglycemia mimics stroke.

interventions.label: Obtain vital signs and initiate cardiopulmonary resuscitation (CPR) if necessary.
Send laboratory tests: complete blood count (CBC), comprehensive metabolic panel (CMP), coagulation studies, toxicology screen
Perform a non-contrast head CT to rule out acute cerebrovascular event or traumatic brain injury
Consult Neurology and Emergency Medicine for further management and potential intubation if airway compromise is suspected
Start fluid resuscitation with normal saline if patient is hypotensive
Oxygen
interventions.why: The patient is unresponsive, which can be a sign of respiratory distress or cardiac arrest. Oxygen is necessary to ensure adequate oxygenation.

interventions.label: IV access ×2
interventions.why: In an emergency situation such as this, large-bore IV access is crucial for rapid administration of fluids and medications.

interventions.label: glucose check
interventions.why: Hypoglycemia can cause altered mental status and seizures. A glucose check is necessary to rule out hypoglycemia as a potential cause.

110.title: Rash
exam: Rash — exam tailored to chief complaint on arrival.

Skin rash morphology and distribution documented.
110.clinical_tip: Petechiae + fever in a sick patient — treat empirically for meningococcemia.
110.objective: Recognize toxic rash syndromes. Resuscitate and cover infection.

interventions.label: Obtain a detailed history, including travel, recent medications, and exposure to potential allergens
Perform a comprehensive physical examination, focusing on the rash's distribution, morphology, and associated symptoms
Take skin scrapings or biopsy samples (with appropriate consent) for microscopic examination, cultures, and pathology
Order complete blood count (CBC), erythrocyte sedimentation rate (ESR), and liver function tests (LFTs)
Consult Dermatology for further evaluation, potential diagnostic testing, and management guidance
Administer topical or systemic antipruritics to manage symptoms and prevent scratching-induced complications
Monitor the patient's vital signs, especially temperature, as some rashes can be associated with fever
Instruct the patient on appropriate skin care and hygiene to prevent infection
Oxygen
interventions.why: The patient presents with a rash, which could potentially require oxygen support if severe distress or AMS occurs.

interventions.label: IV access
interventions.why: Establishing IV access is crucial for administering medications and fluids.

112.title: Found Unconscious
112.diagnosis: Possible diagnoses: Stroke, Severe head injury, Cardiac arrest, Hypoglycemia, or other life-threatening conditions
exam: Unresponsive on arrival, airway and circulation addressed first
Deferred until stable unless trauma pathway
GCS documented; pupils and focal signs assessed
Trauma survey for lacerations, track marks, temperature
112.clinical_tip: Fingerstick glucose before CT — hypoglycemia mimics stroke.

interventions.label: Obtain vital signs (pulse, blood pressure, respiratory rate, oxygen saturation)
Order a complete blood count (CBC), basic metabolic panel (BMP), coagulation profile, glucose level, electrocardiogram (ECG), and computed tomography (CT) scan of the head
Initiate Advanced Cardiac Life Support (ACLS) measures if there are signs of cardiac arrest
Administer intravenous dextrose 50% in case of suspected hypoglycemia
Obtain a detailed history from family or witnesses, if available
Consult Neurology, Neurosurgery, and/or Cardiology as needed based on findings
Oxygen
interventions.why: The patient is found unconscious, indicating potential respiratory distress. Therefore, oxygen is a life-saving intervention that must be ordered immediately.

interventions.label: Order: Order: time-sensitive interventions first; (oxygen, IV access, glucose check)
interventions.why: IV access and glucose check are also necessary for immediate patient stabilization.

116.title: Headache
116.diagnosis: Primary Headache (such as Migraine or Tension-type headache), although other serious conditions like Subarachnoid Hemorrhage cannot be ruled out
116.vitalsText: Initial Vital Signs Day 1 @ 9:00 Temperature: 38.3 C (101.0 degrees F) Pulse: 88 beats/min. Regular rhythm Respiratory rate: 18 /minute Blood pressure, systolic: 118 mm Hg Blood pressure, diastolic: 70 mm Hg Height: 175.3 cm (69.0 in) Weight: 65.8 kg (145 lb) Body Mass Index: 21.4 kg/m2
exam: presents to your office with a an occipital headache, mild vertigo, and a tendency to fall when walking for 6 days.
Abdominal tenderness pattern matches history; no rigid abdomen documented yet.
Mental status intact; cranial nerves and neck stiffness assessed.
Skin rash morphology and distribution documented.
116.clinical_tip: Thunderclap headache = SAH workup. Do not send home without considering red flags.
116.objective: Rule out SAH, meningitis, mass, and hypertensive emergency.

interventions.label: Initial assessment and monitoring for potential signs of serious conditions (e.g., neurological examination, vital signs, and observation)
Imaging (CT or MRI) if the clinical situation warrants it to rule out structural causes such as Subarachnoid Hemorrhage
Oxygen
interventions.why: To provide immediate support for the patient experiencing a headache, which could be a symptom of various critical conditions.

interventions.label: IV access ×2 large-bore
interventions.why: For potential fluid resuscitation and administration of necessary medications.

easy.intro: Initial History Reason(s) for visit: Headache History of present Illness: A 55-year-old female with a past medical history of hypertension presents to your office with a an occipital headache, mild vertigo, and a tendency to fall when walking for 6 days. She states that she never had any problems walking or being off-balance. Eight days ago, she stated that she had a pink rash on her arms and torso and felt feverish. The rash improved after a couple days, but the headache and tendency to fall persisted. She had nausea, but she denied any vomiting. She feels weak and "under the weather." She denies travel, but went hiking 2 weeks ago nearby her house which she routinely does every summer. She denies any history of migraines. She will occasionally get a tension headache which she will treat with Ibuprofen. She has never had a headache that has lasted this long. She denies any trauma to her head. She denies any sick contacts. She denies any recent antibiotic use. She is very concerned about her symptoms. PMH: Past Medical History: Hypertension. The patient recently had a colonoscopy screening which was normal. Current medications: HCTZ 25mg PO daily Allergies: None Vaccinations: Received Childhood vaccinations. Women's health: The patient cannot remember when her last pap smear was done. Family History: Her father has a history of Hypertension and died of a stroke. Her mother has no health problems and is still living. Social History: Marital/Family: She is married with one child. She lives in Gaithersburg, Maryland. Personal habits: The patient denies smoking. She lives an active lifestyle. She drinks alcohol socially. She denies use of illegal drugs or misuse of medications. Occupation/educational: She works as a public relations specialist. She is a college graduate with a BS in communication. Recreational: The patient enjoys to bike and hike. She loves the outdoors and spends as much time as she is able. Review of systems: General: See HPI Skin: See HPI HEENT: Negative Cardiorespiratory: Negative Gastrointestinal: See HPI Genitourinary: Negative Neuropsychiatric: See HPI --- Chart tabs --- * Chart: Progress Notes → presentation_5_Headache_chart_Progress_Notes.png * Chart: Vital Signs → presentation_5_Headache_chart_Vital_Signs.png

117.title: Chest Pain
117.diagnosis: Acute Coronary Syndrome (ACS) or other cardiac conditions are the most likely diagnoses for a patient presenting with unknown chest pain.
117.vitalsText: Initial Vital Signs Day 1 @ 16:00 Temperature: 37.0 C (98.6 degrees F) Pulse: 120 beats/min, Weak Respiratory rate: 34 /minute Blood pressure, systolic: 100 mm Hg Blood pressure, diastolic: 60 mm Hg Height: 183cm (72.0 in) Weight: 97.5 kg (215 lb) Body Mass Index: 29.1 kg/m2
exam: Chest Pain — exam tailored to chief complaint on arrival.
Soft, non-distended, no focal peritoneal signs on initial exam.
Alert and oriented unless perfusion or metabolic derangement present.
No acute rash; capillary refill and perfusion assessed.
117.clinical_tip: Until proven otherwise, treat unstable chest pain as ACS.
117.objective: Rule out life threats. ECG within 10 minutes.

interventions.label: EKG: To evaluate for signs of ischemia or rhythm abnormalities
Troponin level: For diagnosis and monitoring of ACS, including myocardial infarction
Chest X-ray: May help identify conditions such as pneumonia, pulmonary edema, or aortic dissection
Cardiology consult: For further evaluation, management, and potential intervention
Pain management: Administration of pain relievers appropriate for acute chest pain
Oxygen
interventions.why: Chest pain can be a symptom of various conditions, including cardiac ischemia, and oxygen should be provided immediately.

interventions.label: IV access ×2
interventions.why: To administer fluids, medications, and blood products if needed.

interventions.label: order: time-sensitive interventions first
interventions.why: Stabilizing the patient's vital signs is crucial in managing chest pain cases.

easy.intro: Initial History Reason(s) for visit: Chest pain; respiratory distress History of present Illness: The patient, a 65-year-old accountant, is brought to the emergency department by ambulance from the trucking company, where he works. Ten minutes prior to the ambulance’s arrival, the patient developed excruciating, sharp pain on the right side of his chest accompanied by marked respiratory distress. He rates the pain as an 8 on a 10-point scale. The pain increases on respiration, and he is unable to answer questions. A coworker who accompanied the patient to the hospital says that this has never happened to the patient in the past. Coworker mentions patient has a past medical history of emphysema and asthma. Oxygen was administered during transport.

118.title: Muscle Pain
exam: Muscle Pain — exam tailored to chief complaint on arrival.
Soft, non-distended, no focal peritoneal signs on initial exam.
Alert and oriented unless perfusion or metabolic derangement present.
No acute rash; capillary refill and perfusion assessed.
118.clinical_tip: Weakness + hyperreflexia vs hyporeflexia localizes — think electrolytes and cord.
118.objective: Differentiate metabolic, neurologic, and toxic causes.

interventions.label: Obtain laboratory studies (CBC, ESR, CRP, Creatinine kinase, LFT)
Order imaging studies (MRI or Ultrasound for specific areas of pain)
MRI Spine if cord signs
interventions.why: Cord compression is a surgical emergency.

interventions.label: Admit / Neuro consult
interventions.why: Progressive weakness or respiratory concern → ICU.

121.title: Poor Feeding
121.diagnosis: Failure to Thrive (FTT) or Gastroesophageal Reflux Disease (GERD) are common considerations for Poor Feeding of unknown cause in pediatric patients.
exam: Poor Feeding — exam tailored to chief complaint on arrival.
Soft, non-distended, no focal peritoneal signs on initial exam.
Alert and oriented unless perfusion or metabolic derangement present.
Warm skin with fever; no purpura on initial survey.
121.clinical_tip: Febrile infant — sepsis workup, fluids, and close monitoring before disposition.
121.objective: Stabilize the pediatric patient and complete emergent workup before disposition.

interventions.label: Obtain a thorough history, including feeding habits, weight gain, vomiting, and bowel movements
Physical examination to assess growth parameters, signs of dehydration, abdominal distension, or other relevant findings
Investigations such as complete blood count (CBC), chemistry panel, urinalysis, stool studies, and electrolytes to rule out potential underlying causes like infections, metabolic disorders, or anemia
Consider a swallowing study (videofluoroscopy) or pH monitoring if GERD is suspected
Refer the patient to a pediatric gastroenterologist for further evaluation and management
Oxygen
interventions.why: To provide supplemental oxygen and help maintain adequate oxygenation in the patient with poor feeding

interventions.label: IV access x2
interventions.why: To establish two large-bore IV access for fluid administration, medication delivery, and blood draws

interventions.label: glucose check
interventions.why: To quickly assess the blood glucose level of the patient to rule out hypoglycemia as a possible cause of poor feeding

interventions.label: Request an Ankle-Brachial Index (ABI) study
interventions.why: This noninvasive test is used to diagnose PAD by measuring the blood pressure in the arms and legs. A reduced ABI suggests PAD.

interventions.label: Consider ordering electrodiagnostic studies for neuropathy workup if neurological symptoms are present
interventions.why: These tests can help identify nerve damage or dysfunction.

interventions.label: Refer the patient to a vascular surgeon or interventional radiologist for further imaging and potential intervention
Oxygen
interventions.why: To provide immediate support for the patient experiencing leg pain, which may be a sign of an underlying medical condition.

interventions.label: glucose check
interventions.why: To quickly assess blood glucose levels and address any potential hypoglycemia or hyperglycemia.

124.title: Chest Pain
124.diagnosis: Acute Coronary Syndrome (ACS) or other cardiac conditions are the most likely diagnoses in a patient presenting with chest pain of unknown etiology.
124.vitalsText: Initial Vital Signs Day 1 @ 16:00 Temperature: 37.0 C (98.6 degrees F) Pulse: 120 beats/min, Weak Respiratory rate: 34 /minute Blood pressure, systolic: 100 mm Hg Blood pressure, diastolic: 60 mm Hg Height: 183cm (72.0 in) Weight: 97.5 kg (215 lb) Body Mass Index: 29.1 kg/m2
exam: Chest Pain — exam tailored to chief complaint on arrival.
Soft, non-distended, no focal peritoneal signs on initial exam.
Alert and oriented unless perfusion or metabolic derangement present.
No acute rash; capillary refill and perfusion assessed.
124.clinical_tip: Until proven otherwise, treat unstable chest pain as ACS.
124.objective: Rule out life threats. ECG within 10 minutes.

interventions.label: Obtain immediate ECG
interventions.why: To evaluate for signs of ischemia, such as ST-segment elevations, depression, or T-wave inversions

interventions.label: Order serum biomarkers such as Troponin I/T
interventions.why: To assess myocardial injury and help confirm a diagnosis of ACS

interventions.label: Admit the patient for continuous monitoring and further investigation
interventions.why: Given the unclear etiology, close observation is necessary to identify any signs of acute decompensation or progression

interventions.label: Consult Cardiology
interventions.why: For expert advice on management strategies and potential interventions such as PCI or CABG

interventions.label: Prescribe aspirin (unless contraindicated) and other antiplatelet agents as indicated
interventions.why: To reduce the risk of thrombosis, which is a common complication in ACS

interventions.label: Oxygen
interventions.why: The patient is experiencing chest pain, which could be a life-threatening condition. Oxygen is essential to ensure adequate oxygenation.

interventions.label: IV access ×2
interventions.why: Establishing IV access is crucial for administering medications and fluids quickly if needed.

interventions.label: glucose check
interventions.why: Hypoglycemia can cause symptoms similar to chest pain, so it's important to rule this out early.

easy.intro: Initial History Reason(s) for visit: Chest pain; respiratory distress History of present Illness: The patient, a 65-year-old accountant, is brought to the emergency department by ambulance from the trucking company, where he works. Ten minutes prior to the ambulance’s arrival, the patient developed excruciating, sharp pain on the right side of his chest accompanied by marked respiratory distress. He rates the pain as an 8 on a 10-point scale. The pain increases on respiration, and he is unable to answer questions. A coworker who accompanied the patient to the hospital says that this has never happened to the patient in the past. Coworker mentions patient has a past medical history of emphysema and asthma. Oxygen was administered during transport.

interventions.label: Obtain ECG
interventions.why: ECG can provide initial information about the possible cause of chest pain, such as ischemia or arrhythmia.

interventions.label: Order cardiac enzymes (troponin and CK-MB)
interventions.why: Cardiac enzyme levels can indicate myocardial damage, which is suggestive of ACS.

interventions.label: Oxygen
interventions.why: Chest pain can be a symptom of various life-threatening conditions, and immediate oxygen administration is crucial to support respiration.

interventions.label: IV access ×2
interventions.why: Large-bore IV access is essential for administering fluids and medications quickly in case of an emergency.

interventions.label: glucose check
interventions.why: Hypoglycemia can cause or mimic symptoms of chest pain.

I rates the pain as an 8 on a 10-point scale. The pain increases on respiration, and he is unable to answer questions. A coworker who accompanied I to the hospital says that this has never happened to I in the past. Coworker mentions patient has a past medical history of emphysema and asthma. Oxygen was administered during transport.

126.title: Shortness of Breath
exam: Dyspneic, speaking in short phrases if severe
126.clinical_tip: ABG or pulse ox + ECG + CXR in the first minutes.
126.objective: Stabilize hypoxia. Differentiate pulmonary vs cardiac vs metabolic.

interventions.label: Obtain arterial blood gases (ABG)
interventions.why: To assess the level of oxygenation and acid-base status

interventions.label: Chest X-ray
interventions.why: To evaluate for pulmonary edema, pneumonia, or consolidation

interventions.label: Echocardiogram
interventions.why: To evaluate cardiac function and identify any structural heart disease

interventions.label: D-dimer level
interventions.why: To help rule out deep vein thrombosis (DVT) or pulmonary embolism

interventions.label: Troponin I or T
interventions.why: To rule out acute coronary syndrome

interventions.label: Complete blood count (CBC)
interventions.why: To evaluate for anemia, infection, or inflammation

interventions.label: Coagulation studies
interventions.why: To help rule out DVT or pulmonary embolism

interventions.label: CPAP or BiPAP if respiratory distress is present
interventions.why: To provide oxygenation and ventilation support

interventions.label: Cardiology consult
interventions.why: For further evaluation and management of potential cardiac causes

interventions.label: Internal medicine consult
interventions.why: For further evaluation and management of potential non-cardiac causes

interventions.label: Oxygen
interventions.why: Immediate oxygen delivery is crucial for patients experiencing shortness of breath.

interventions.label: IV access ×2
interventions.why: Large-bore IV access is necessary to administer fluids, medications, and blood products.

127.title: Rash
exam: Rash — exam tailored to chief complaint on arrival.
Soft, non-distended, no focal peritoneal signs on initial exam.
Alert and oriented unless perfusion or metabolic derangement present.
Skin rash morphology and distribution documented.
127.clinical_tip: Stool guaiac and blood cultures are required red items in pediatrics.
127.objective: Isolated thrombocytopenia workup; treat if bleeding or very low platelets.

interventions.label: Office or ED as acuity dictates
CBC
interventions.why: Isolated low platelets after viral illness.

interventions.label: Peripheral smear
interventions.why: Rule out pseudothrombocytopenia and other causes.

interventions.label: Observation or IVIG / steroids
interventions.why: Treat if platelets <20k or active bleeding.

128.title: Depression
128.diagnosis: Major Depressive Disorder is the most likely diagnosis for a patient presenting with Depression of unknown etiology.
exam: Depression — exam tailored to chief complaint on arrival.
Soft, non-distended, no focal peritoneal signs on initial exam.
Alert and oriented unless perfusion or metabolic derangement present.
No acute rash; capillary refill and perfusion assessed.
128.clinical_tip: Stabilize first — ABCs, IV access, monitor, then targeted workup.
128.objective: Identify and treat life threats within the time limit.

interventions.label: Psychiatric Consult
interventions.why: To assess the severity of depression, rule out other psychiatric disorders, and guide further management.

interventions.label: Laboratory workup (CBC, Chemistry Panel, TSH, Urinalysis)
Psychological Evaluation
interventions.why: To assess the patient's psychological state and functioning, which can aid in diagnosis and treatment planning.

129.title: Facial Pain
129.diagnosis: Trigeminal neuralgia or other neuropathic facial pain (due to facial pain presentation)
exam: Facial Pain — exam tailored to chief complaint on arrival.
Soft, non-distended, no focal peritoneal signs on initial exam.
Alert and oriented unless perfusion or metabolic derangement present.
Warm skin with fever; no purpura on initial survey.
129.clinical_tip: Fingerstick glucose before CT — hypoglycemia mimics stroke.

interventions.label: Obtain a detailed history, focusing on the onset, duration, and characteristics of the pain
Perform a neurological examination, including cranial nerve evaluation
Order MRI or CT with contrast to evaluate for any structural lesions in the trigeminal nerve region
Consider referral to a neurologist or neurosurgeon for further evaluation and management if MRI/CT reveals abnormalities
Initiate pain management as needed, such as carbamazepine or gabapentin, while awaiting specialist consultation
Oxygen
interventions.why: To provide immediate oxygen support for the patient experiencing facial pain.

interventions.label: IV access x2
interventions.why: To establish large-bore IV access in preparation for fluid resuscitation and administration of medications.

easy.clinicalTip: Teaching hint: Fingerstick glucose before CT — hypoglycemia mimics stroke.
easy.objective: Stabilize ABCs. Identify reversible causes. Start with stabilization, then targeted testing.

standard.objective: Stabilize ABCs. Identify reversible causes.

130.title: Cough
130.diagnosis: Possible Pneumonia, Bronchitis, or Asthma (based on cough as the chief complaint)
exam: Cough — exam tailored to chief complaint on arrival.
Soft, non-distended, no focal peritoneal signs on initial exam.
Alert and oriented unless perfusion or metabolic derangement present.
No acute rash; capillary refill and perfusion assessed.
130.clinical_tip: Until proven otherwise, treat unstable chest pain as ACS.
130.objective: Rule out life threats. ECG within 10 minutes.

interventions.label: Order complete blood count (CBC) and basic metabolic panel
Administer oxygen therapy if oxygen saturation levels are low
Start broad-spectrum antibiotics empirically (if fever, purulent sputum, or signs of bacterial infection)
interventions.why: To address potential bacterial infections that could be causing the cough

interventions.label: Consider pulmonology consultation for further evaluation and management
Monitor respiratory status and vital signs
CMP/BMP
interventions.why: This bundle includes electrolytes, renal function, and liver function tests which are important to evaluate a patient with an unknown diagnosis. It also includes urinalysis to evaluate for potential infections or other causes of cough.

interventions.label: UA ± culture
interventions.why: Urinalysis can help identify urinary tract infections (UTI) as a possible cause of the patient's cough.

easy.clinicalTip: Teaching hint: Until proven otherwise, treat unstable chest pain as ACS.
easy.objective: Rule out life threats. ECG within 10 minutes. Start with stabilization, then targeted testing.

131.title: Abdominal Pain
131.vitalsText: Initial Vital Signs Day 1 @ 18:00 Temperature: 37.2 C (98.9 degrees F) Pulse: 106 beat/min, Tachycardic Respiratory rate: 20 /minute Blood pressure, systolic: 114 mm Hg Blood pressure, diastolic: 79 mm Hg Height: 160.0 cm (63.0 in) Weight: 80.5 kg (177.4 lb) Body Mass Index: 31.4 kg/m2
exam: presents to the emergency room with abdominal pain, lethargy, and vomiting x2 days.
Abdominal tenderness pattern matches history; no rigid abdomen documented yet.
Alert and oriented unless perfusion or metabolic derangement present.
No acute rash; capillary refill and perfusion assessed.
131.clinical_tip: Elderly and immunocompromised may have minimal exam findings until rupture.
131.objective: Resuscitate, image, involve surgery early if indicated.

interventions.label: Order abdominal ultrasound or CT scan
Consider obtaining electrocardiogram (ECG) to rule out ischemic heart disease
CBC · Lipase · LFTs · UA
interventions.why: Narrow differential: biliary, pancreatic, UTI, medical.

interventions.label: CT Abdomen/Pelvis
interventions.why: High yield for appendicitis, diverticulitis, obstruction.

interventions.label: Antibiotics if indicated
interventions.why: Give before OR if sepsis or perforation suspected.

easy.intro: Initial History Reason(s) for visit: Abdominal Pain History of present Illness: A 22-year-old female presents to the emergency room with abdominal pain, lethargy, and vomiting x2 days. Her vomiting is non-bloody. Her abdominal pain is diffuse, which she rates as a 6/10. She also claims to have had an increased appetite prior to having these symptoms. She also claims that she’s been developing blurry vision, dizziness, and drowsiness. She also states that she is having increased urination Past Medical History Past Medical History: None Current medications: Birth control Allergies: None Vaccinations: The patient received her childhood vaccinations. She has never received any HPV vaccines. Women's health: The patient cannot remember when her last pap smear was done. Family History: The patient has a brother with Diabetes. Social History: Marital/Family: Single. She is an undergraduate student at a local university. Personal habits: The patient denies smoking. She drinks socially. She smoked marijuana "a few times" with her friends. She denies other illicit drugs or misuse of medications. Occupation/educational: She is a student at a local university Recreational: She plays the piano and sings in a band. Review of systems: General: See HPI Skin: Her skin is dry. HEENT: Her throat is dry. Cardiorespiratory: She feels that her heart is beating faster. interventions.why: Wrist and Ankle Pain could be due to various causes such as trauma, infection, or systemic conditions. Therefore, the first step should be to ensure oxygen supply and IV access.

interventions.label: Order: Order: time-sensitive interventions first; (oxygen, IV access, glucose check)

140.diagnosis: Primary Headache Disorders (such as Migraine, Tension-type headache) or Secondary Headaches (like Intracranial bleed or Brain tumor) should be considered
140.vitalsText: Initial Vital Signs Day 1 @ 9:00 Temperature: 38.3 C (101.0 degrees F) Pulse: 88 beats/min. Regular rhythm Respiratory rate: 18 /minute Blood pressure, systolic: 118 mm Hg Blood pressure, diastolic: 70 mm Hg Height: 175.3 cm (69.0 in) Weight: 65.8 kg (145 lb) Body Mass Index: 21.4 kg/m2
exam: presents to your office with a an occipital headache, mild vertigo, and a tendency to fall when walking for 6 days.
Abdominal tenderness pattern matches history; no rigid abdomen documented yet.
Mental status intact; cranial nerves and neck stiffness assessed.
Skin rash morphology and distribution documented.
140.clinical_tip: Thunderclap headache = SAH workup. Do not send home without considering red flags.
140.objective: Rule out SAH, meningitis, mass, and hypertensive emergency.

interventions.label: Perform a detailed physical examination focusing on neurological assessment
Order laboratory tests: Complete Blood Count, Electrolytes, Glucose, and Coagulation profile
Order imaging studies: Non-contrast CT head if patient is acutely ill or has focal neurologic signs, MRI brain for further evaluation when CT is normal or indications suggest
Consider EEG if seizure activity suspected
If patient reports migraine-like symptoms (photophobia, phonophobia, nausea), consider prophylactic treatment with a non-narcotic analgesic or anti-emetic
Consult Neurology if diagnosis remains unclear, for further evaluation and management
CMP/BMP
interventions.why: To assess the patient's electrolytes, renal function, and liver function.

interventions.label: UA
interventions.why: To rule out a urinary tract infection or other potential causes of the headache.

easy.intro: Initial History Reason(s) for visit: Headache History of present Illness: A 55-year-old female with a past medical history of hypertension presents to your office with a an occipital headache, mild vertigo, and a tendency to fall when walking for 6 days. She states that she never had any problems walking or being off-balance. Eight days ago, she stated that she had a pink rash on her arms and torso and felt feverish. The rash improved after a couple days, but the headache and tendency to fall persisted. She had nausea, but she denied any vomiting. She feels weak and "under the weather." She denies travel, but went hiking 2 weeks ago nearby her house which she routinely does every summer. She denies any history of migraines. She will occasionally get a tension headache which she will treat with Ibuprofen. She has never had a headache that has lasted this long. She denies any trauma to her head. She denies any sick contacts. She denies any recent antibiotic use. She is very concerned about her symptoms. PMH: Past Medical History: Hypertension. The patient recently had a colonoscopy screening which was normal. Current medications: HCTZ 25mg PO daily Allergies: None Vaccinations: Received Childhood vaccinations. Women's health: The patient cannot remember when her last pap smear was done. Family History: Her father has a history of Hypertension and died of a stroke. Her mother has no health problems and is still living. Social History: Marital/Family: She is married with one child. She lives in Gaithersburg, Maryland. Personal habits: The patient denies smoking. She lives an active lifestyle. She drinks alcohol socially. She denies use of illegal drugs or misuse of medications. Occupation/educational: She works as a public relations specialist. She is a college graduate with a BS in communication. Recreational: The patient enjoys to bike and hike. She loves the outdoors and spends as much time as she is able. She will occasionally get a tension headache which she will treat with Ibuprofen. She has never had a headache that has lasted this long. She denies any trauma to her head. She denies any sick contacts. She denies any recent antibiotic use. She is very concerned about her symptoms. PMH: Past Medical History: Hypertension. The patient recently had a colonoscopy screening which was normal. Current medications: HCTZ 25mg PO daily Allergies: None Vaccinations: Received Childhood vaccinations. Women's health: The patient cannot remember when her last pap smear was done. Family History: Her father has a history of Hypertension and died of a stroke. Her mother has no health problems and is still living. Social History: Marital/Family: She is married with one child. She lives in Gaithersburg, Maryland. Personal habits: The patient denies smoking. She lives an active lifestyle. She drinks alcohol socially. She denies use of illegal drugs or misuse of medications. Occupation/educational: She works as a public relations specialist. She is a college graduate with a BS in communication. Recreational: The patient enjoys to bike and hike. She loves the outdoors and spends as much time as she is able. 141.title: Rash
exam: Rash — exam tailored to chief complaint on arrival.
Soft, non-distended, no focal peritoneal signs on initial exam.
Alert and oriented unless perfusion or metabolic derangement present.
Skin rash morphology and distribution documented.
141.clinical_tip: Petechiae + fever in a sick patient — treat empirically for meningococcemia.
141.objective: Recognize toxic rash syndromes. Resuscitate and cover infection.

interventions.label: Obtain a detailed history, including onset, location, distribution, and characteristics of the rash.
Perform physical examination with dermatological assessment.
Order laboratory tests: Complete blood count (CBC), liver function tests (LFTs), renal function tests (RFTs), urinalysis, and blood cultures if fever is present.
Consider skin biopsy or other specialized tests such as direct immunofluorescence or PCR for specific etiologies based on clinical suspicion.

If the rash is associated with systemic symptoms like fever, malaise, or lymphadenopathy, consider empiric antibiotic therapy pending culture results (e.g., broad-spectrum penicillin or cephalosporin) but avoid administering antihistamines and steroids until the cause is determined.
Consult dermatology for further evaluation and management.
CMP/BMP
interventions.why: This bundle includes the necessary lab tests to check for electrolytes, renal function, and liver function which can be useful in determining the underlying cause of a rash.

interventions.label: Obtain a thorough history, including maternal and child health, growth and development, feeding habits, and family dynamics.
interventions.why: To gather information that may help narrow down the diagnosis of the underlying cause of poor feeding.

interventions.label: Perform physical examination focusing on growth parameters, developmental milestones, signs of malnutrition (e.g., edema, skin changes), and any underlying conditions.
interventions.why: To assess the patient's overall health status, identify any abnormalities that may contribute to poor feeding, and monitor progress during treatment.

interventions.label: Oxygen
interventions.why: Immediate oxygen delivery for poor feeding is important to address any potential respiratory distress.

interventions.label: IV access ×2
interventions.why: Two large-bore IV access will allow for quick administration of fluids and medications if needed.

interventions.label: glucose check
interventions.why: A glucose check can help rule out hypoglycemia, which can cause poor feeding in infants.

146.title: Found Unconscious
146.diagnosis: Traumatic Brain Injury (TBI) until proven otherwise
exam: Unresponsive on arrival, airway and circulation addressed first
Deferred until stable unless trauma pathway
GCS documented; pupils and focal signs assessed
Trauma survey for lacerations, track marks, temperature
146.clinical_tip: Fingerstick glucose before CT — hypoglycemia mimics stroke.

interventions.label: Obtain vital signs and initiate basic life support if necessary.
interventions.why: To assess the patient's immediate condition and provide appropriate resuscitation.

interventions.label: Obtain a detailed history including mechanism of injury, LOC, Glasgow Coma Scale (GCS), past medical history, medications, and allergies.
interventions.why: To understand the specifics of the trauma and any pre-existing conditions that may affect treatment.

interventions.label: Obtain physical examination focusing on neurological assessment.
interventions.why: To assess for signs of TBI or other neurological disorders.

interventions.label: Immediate imaging studies: Non-contrast head CT to assess for intracranial hemorrhage or other structural abnormalities.
interventions.why: To provide a visual assessment of the patient's brain and identify potential injuries.

interventions.label: Consult Neurosurgery if GCS is less than 15, signs of increased ICP, or if there are associated skull fractures.
interventions.why: For potential surgery to relieve pressure on the brain or manage any fractures.

interventions.label: Obtain labs such as complete blood count (CBC), coagulation profile, electrolytes, and glucose.
interventions.why: To evaluate for possible complications related to TBI, such as infection or bleeding.

interventions.label: Admit to an intensive care unit for close monitoring.
interventions.why: To provide constant monitoring of the patient's condition and quick response in case of deterioration.

interventions.label: Oxygen
interventions.why: The patient is found unconscious, and immediate oxygen administration is necessary to ensure adequate ventilation.

interventions.label: IV access ×2
interventions.why: Immediate establishment of IV access is crucial for administering fluids, medications, and blood products if needed.

easy.clinicalTip: Teaching hint: Fingerstick glucose before CT — hypoglycemia mimics stroke.
easy.objective: Stabilize ABCs. Identify reversible causes. Start with stabilization, then targeted testing.

exam: Chest Pain — exam tailored to chief complaint on arrival.

148.clinical_tip: Pleuritic pain days–weeks post-MI; pain better leaning forward. Avoid anticoag if large effusion.

easy.intro: Initial History Reason(s) for visit: Chest pain; respiratory distress History of present Illness: The patient, a 65-year-old accountant, is brought to the emergency department by ambulance from the trucking company, where he works. Ten minutes prior to the ambulance’s arrival, the patient developed excruciating, sharp pain on the right side of his chest accompanied by marked respiratory distress. He rates the pain as an 8 on a 10-point scale. The pain increases on respiration, and he is unable to answer questions. A coworker who accompanied the patient to the hospital says that this has never happened to the patient in the past. Coworker mentions patient has a past medical history of emphysema and asthma. Oxygen was administered during transport.

149.title: Chronic Diarrhea
149.diagnosis: Inflammatory Bowel Disease (IBD) or Infectious Diarrhea (based on the history and exclusion of other causes)
exam: Chronic Diarrhea — exam tailored to chief complaint on arrival.
Soft, non-distended, no focal peritoneal signs on initial exam.
Alert and oriented unless perfusion or metabolic derangement present.
Warm skin with fever; no purpura on initial survey.
149.clinical_tip: Elderly and immunocompromised may have minimal exam findings until rupture.
149.objective: Resuscitate, image, involve surgery early if indicated.

interventions.label: Obtain stool sample for culture, Clostridium difficile toxin, ova and parasites, and C. difficile PCR
Consider fecal calprotectin level as an inflammatory marker
Imaging: abdominal CT or MRI may be considered if symptoms persist or worsen, but usually colonoscopy is preferred for further evaluation
Consult Gastroenterology for possible endoscopic evaluation (colonoscopy) and biopsy
Antibiotics should only be given based on culture results to avoid overuse and potential development of resistant organisms
order1
interventions.why: Upper GI Bleed, which is the closest match to this case, includes orders for oxygen, monitor, IV access ×2, pulse ox; IV fluids, type & cross, NPO; CBC, CMP, coags, CXR; however, it also includes an order for GI consult for urgent EGD, which is not typically necessary in the initial management of chronic diarrhea.

150.title: Back Pain and Fatigue
exam: Back Pain and Fatigue — exam tailored to chief complaint on arrival.
Soft, non-distended, no focal peritoneal signs on initial exam.
Alert and oriented unless perfusion or metabolic derangement present.
No acute rash; capillary refill and perfusion assessed.
150.clinical_tip: Order UPEP — UA dipstick misses Bence-Jones protein. Skeletal survey, NOT bone scan.
150.objective: CRAB features workup and hematology referral.

interventions.label: Office or ward
interventions.why: Bone pain, fatigue, weight loss in elderly.

152.title: Headache
152.vitalsText: Initial Vital Signs Day 1 @ 9:00 Temperature: 38.3 C (101.0 degrees F) Pulse: 88 beats/min. Regular rhythm Respiratory rate: 18 /minute Blood pressure, systolic: 118 mm Hg Blood pressure, diastolic: 70 mm Hg Height: 175.3 cm (69.0 in) Weight: 65.8 kg (145 lb) Body Mass Index: 21.4 kg/m2
exam: presents to your office with a an occipital headache, mild vertigo, and a tendency to fall when walking for 6 days.
Abdominal tenderness pattern matches history; no rigid abdomen documented yet.
Mental status intact; cranial nerves and neck stiffness assessed.
Skin rash morphology and distribution documented.
152.clinical_tip: Family with flu-like headaches in winter — think CO; 100% O₂ first.
152.objective: Same as Case 151 — high-flow O₂ and CO level.

interventions.label: 100% O₂ non-rebreather
interventions.why: Do not wait for lab to start treatment.

interventions.label: Cardiac monitor + pulse ox
interventions.why: Note: pulse ox unreliable in CO.

easy.intro: Initial History Reason(s) for visit: Headache History of present Illness: A 55-year-old female with a past medical history of hypertension presents to your office with a an occipital headache, mild vertigo, and a tendency to fall when walking for 6 days. She states that she never had any problems walking or being off-balance. Eight days ago, she stated that she had a pink rash on her arms and torso and felt feverish. The rash improved after a couple days, but the headache and tendency to fall persisted. She had nausea, but she denied any vomiting. She feels weak and "under the weather." She denies travel, but went hiking 2 weeks ago nearby her house which she routinely does every summer. She denies any history of migraines. She will occasionally get a tension headache which she will treat with Ibuprofen. She has never had a headache that has lasted this long. She denies any trauma to her head. She denies any sick contacts. She denies any recent antibiotic use. She is very concerned about her symptoms. PMH: Past Medical History: Hypertension. The patient recently had a colonoscopy screening which was normal. Current medications: HCTZ 25mg PO daily Allergies: None Vaccinations: Received Childhood vaccinations. Women's health: The patient cannot remember when her last pap smear was done. Family History: Her father has a history of Hypertension and died of a stroke. Her mother has no health problems and is still living. Social History: Marital/Family: She is married with one child. She lives in Gaithersburg, Maryland. Personal habits: The patient denies smoking. She lives an active lifestyle. She drinks alcohol socially. She denies use of illegal drugs or misuse of medications. Occupation/educational: She works as a public relations specialist. She is a college graduate with a BS in communication. Recreational: The patient enjoys to bike and hike. She loves the outdoors and spends as much time as she is able. 155.title: Shortness of breath
155.diagnosis: Acute respiratory distress syndrome (ARDS) or pulmonary edema as potential causes of shortness of breath in an undiagnosed patient
exam: Dyspneic, speaking in short phrases if severe
155.clinical_tip: ABG or pulse ox + ECG + CXR in the first minutes.

interventions.label: Obtain vital signs, including oxygen saturation and blood pressure
interventions.why: Essential for initial assessment of patient's condition

interventions.label: Perform chest X-ray to evaluate lung fields
interventions.why: Useful in identifying potential causes such as pneumonia, pleural effusion, or pulmonary edema

interventions.label: Order arterial blood gas (ABG) analysis for oxygenation status and acid-base balance
interventions.why: Critical for assessing the patient's respiratory and metabolic status

interventions.label: Administer supplemental oxygen as needed to maintain oxygen saturation above 90%
interventions.why: Important for preventing further deterioration in the patient's respiratory status

interventions.label: Initiate diuretics if signs of heart failure are present or pulmonary edema is suspected
interventions.why: Helps manage fluid overload and reduce symptoms associated with pulmonary edema

interventions.label: Consider obtaining echocardiogram to assess cardiac function
interventions.why: Helpful in ruling out cardiogenic causes of dyspnea

interventions.label: Consult Internal Medicine, Pulmonology, and Intensive Care Unit (ICU) as needed based on the patient's condition
Oxygen
interventions.why: The patient is experiencing shortness of breath, making oxygen a necessary intervention.

interventions.label: IV access ×2 large-bore
interventions.why: In cases where the patient's condition may worsen rapidly or require emergency treatment, having large-bore IV access is crucial.

interventions.label: Perform comprehensive physical exam including vital signs, weight, and lymph node evaluation
Order laboratory tests: Complete blood count (CBC), urinalysis, serum electrolytes, lipid profile, liver function tests, renal function tests, thyroid function tests
Order imaging studies if needed: Chest X-ray, echocardiogram, ultrasound, MRI/CT scan
Consult Internal Medicine or Nephrology based on lab results and clinical presentation
Prescribe diuretics if heart failure suspected (if patient is stable enough) or refer for specialist management
Refer to physical therapy for lymphatic drainage if diagnosis of Lymphedema is confirmed
Oxygen
interventions.why: The patient is experiencing generalized swelling which could indicate a life-threatening condition. Immediate oxygen administration and establishing IV access are critical to stabilize the patient.

interventions.label: IV access ×2 large-bore
interventions.why: Large-bore IV access is essential for administering fluids quickly and potentially lifesaving medications.

158.title: Knee Pain
158.diagnosis: Proximal Tibiofibular Joint Sprain, Patellofemoral Pain Syndrome, or Meniscal Tear (common causes of knee pain)

158.clinical_tip: Weakness + hyperreflexia vs hyporeflexia localizes — think electrolytes and cord.
158.objective: Differentiate metabolic, neurologic, and toxic causes.

interventions.label: Obtain a detailed history and perform a physical examination
interventions.why: This is the first step in diagnosing knee pain. It allows for a comprehensive understanding of the patient's symptoms, their onset, and any aggravating or alleviating factors.

interventions.label: Order X-rays to rule out fractures or other bony abnormalities
interventions.why: X-rays can help identify fractures, osteochondritis dissecans, avascular necrosis, or other bone disorders that may cause knee pain.

interventions.label: Consider ordering an MRI if symptoms persist or worsen, or in cases of severe trauma
interventions.why: MRI is useful for diagnosing soft tissue injuries such as ligament sprains, meniscal tears, and cartilage damage.

interventions.label: If the diagnosis is still uncertain, consider a knee aspiration and synovial fluid analysis
interventions.why: This can help identify inflammatory conditions or infections that may be causing the knee pain.

interventions.label: CMP/BMP
interventions.why: This bundle includes electrolytes, renal function, and liver function tests that can help in the diagnosis of various conditions causing knee pain. It also includes a urinalysis (UA) to check for any potential infection or urinary issues.

161.title: Headache
161.diagnosis: Subarachnoid Hemorrhage, Migraine, Tension Headache (considering unknown diagnosis), Brain Tumor, Intracranial Infection
161.vitalsText: Initial Vital Signs Day 1 @ 9:00 Temperature: 38.3 C (101.0 degrees F) Pulse: 88 beats/min. Regular rhythm Respiratory rate: 18 /minute Blood pressure, systolic: 118 mm Hg Blood pressure, diastolic: 70 mm Hg Height: 175.3 cm (69.0 in) Weight: 65.8 kg (145 lb) Body Mass Index: 21.4 kg/m2
exam: presents to your office with a an occipital headache, mild vertigo, and a tendency to fall when walking for 6 days.
Abdominal tenderness pattern matches history; no rigid abdomen documented yet.
Mental status intact; cranial nerves and neck stiffness assessed.
Skin rash morphology and distribution documented.
161.clinical_tip: Thunderclap headache = SAH workup. Do not send home without considering red flags.
161.objective: Rule out SAH, meningitis, mass, and hypertensive emergency.

interventions.label: Obtain urgent CT head with contrast
interventions.why: To rule out conditions such as subarachnoid hemorrhage, brain tumor, and intracranial infection that may require emergent intervention

interventions.label: Order lumbar puncture for cerebrospinal fluid analysis if CT is negative or inconclusive
interventions.why: To further evaluate the cause of headache if CT results are unclear or negative

interventions.label: Evaluate for secondary causes of headache (e.g., hypertension, diabetes mellitus, thyroid disorder)
Consult Neurology and Neurosurgery as necessary
interventions.why: For specialized evaluation and management of potential neurological conditions

interventions.label: Oxygen
interventions.why: The patient is experiencing a headache, which could be a sign of various conditions such as meningitis, hypertensive emergency, or seizure. Providing oxygen immediately helps to stabilize the patient.

interventions.label: IV access ×2 large-bore
interventions.why: Ensuring IV access with large-bore catheters is crucial for prompt administration of fluids and medications in case of an emergency or if the patient's condition worsens.

easy.intro: Initial History Reason(s) for visit: Headache History of present Illness: A 55-year-old female with a past medical history of hypertension presents to your office with a an occipital headache, mild vertigo, and a tendency to fall when walking for 6 days. She states that she never had any problems walking or being off-balance. Eight days ago, she stated that she had a pink rash on her arms and torso and felt feverish. The rash improved after a couple days, but the headache and tendency to fall persisted. She had nausea, but she denied any vomiting. She feels weak and "under the weather." She denies travel, but went hiking 2 weeks ago nearby her house which she routinely does every summer. She denies any history of migraines. She will occasionally get a tension headache which she will treat with Ibuprofen. She has never had a headache that has lasted this long. She denies any trauma to her head. She denies any sick contacts. She denies any recent antibiotic use. She is very concerned about her symptoms. PMH: Past Medical History: Hypertension. The patient recently had a colonoscopy screening which was normal. Current medications: HCTZ 25mg PO daily Allergies: None Vaccinations: Received Childhood vaccinations. Women's health: The patient cannot remember when her last pap smear was done. Family History: Her father has a history of Hypertension and died of a stroke. Her mother has no health problems and is still living. Social History: Marital/Family: She is married with one child. She lives in Gaithersburg, Maryland. Personal habits: The patient denies smoking. She lives an active lifestyle. She drinks alcohol socially. She denies use of illegal drugs or misuse of medications. Occupation/educational: She works as a public relations specialist. She is a college graduate with a BS in communication. Recreational: The patient enjoys to bike and hike. She loves the outdoors and spends as much time as she is able. hard.intro: Initial History Reason(s) for visit: Headache History of present Illness: A 55-year-old female with a past medical history of hypertension presents to your office with a an occipital headache, mild vertigo, and a tendency to fall when walking for 6 days. She states that she never had any problems walking or being off-balance. Eight days ago, she stated that she had a pink rash on her arms and torso and felt feverish. The rash improved after a couple days, but the headache and tendency to fall persisted. She had nausea, but she denied any vomiting. She feels weak and "under the weather." She denies travel, but went hiking 2 weeks ago nearby her house which she routinely does every summer. She denies any history of migraines. She will occasionally get a tension headache which she will treat with Ibuprofen. She has never had a headache that has lasted this long. She denies any trauma to her head. She denies any sick contacts. She denies any recent antibiotic use. She is very concerned about her symptoms. PMH: Past Medical History: Hypertension. 162.title: Pain During Urination
162.diagnosis: Acute Cystitis or Urinary Tract Infection (UTI) is a common consideration for pain during urination with unknown diagnosis.
exam: Pain During Urination — exam tailored to chief complaint on arrival.
Soft, non-distended, no focal peritoneal signs on initial exam.
Alert and oriented unless perfusion or metabolic derangement present.
Warm skin with fever; no purpura on initial survey.
162.clinical_tip: Complicated UTI in men, pregnancy, or fever → admit and image.
162.objective: Treat infection; rule out pyelonephritis and STI.

interventions.label: Laboratory tests: Urinalysis, Urine Culture and Sensitivity
Imaging studies: Ultrasound of the kidneys, ureters, and bladder (US KUB) if patient's condition warrants imaging
CMP/BMP
interventions.why: To assess renal and liver function and screen for potential infections.

interventions.label: UA
interventions.why: To check for hematuria, proteinuria or signs of a urinary tract infection.

easy.clinicalTip: Teaching hint: Complicated UTI in men, pregnancy, or fever → admit and image.
easy.objective: Treat infection; rule out pyelonephritis and STI. Start with stabilization, then targeted testing.

163.title: Ear Pain
exam: Ear Pain — exam tailored to chief complaint on arrival.
Soft, non-distended, no focal peritoneal signs on initial exam.
Alert and oriented unless perfusion or metabolic derangement present.
No acute rash; capillary refill and perfusion assessed.
163.clinical_tip: Office or home — placing simple AOM in the ED loses points.

interventions.label: Office or home (not ED)
interventions.why: Stable pediatric ear pain — wrong location deducts points.

easy.clinicalTip: Teaching hint: Office or home — placing simple AOM in the ED loses points.

164.title: Pelvic Pain
164.diagnosis: Pelvic Inflammatory Disease (PID) or other gynecologic causes, appendicitis, ovarian torsion, or other abdominal pathologies are possibilities given pelvic pain of unknown cause in a reproductive-aged female. However, it's important to note that the final diagnosis may require further evaluation.
164.vitalsText: Initial Vital Signs Day 1 @ 9:00 Temperature: 37.0 C (98.6 degrees F) Pulse: 110 beat/min, Tachycardic Respiratory rate: 22 /minute Blood pressure, systolic: 140 mm Hg Blood pressure, diastolic: 92 mm Hg Height: 175.3 cm (69.0 in) Weight: 65.8 kg (145 lb) Body Mass Index: 21.4 kg/m2
exam: complaining of pelvic pain for the last month.
Abdominal tenderness pattern matches history; no rigid abdomen documented yet.
Alert and oriented unless perfusion or metabolic derangement present.
No acute rash; capillary refill and perfusion assessed.
164.clinical_tip: Positive pregnancy test changes everything — ectopic until proven otherwise.
164.objective: Rule out ectopic, torsion, and rupture.

interventions.label: Laboratory tests: Complete blood count (CBC), C-reactive protein (CRP), urinalysis, and urine culture
Imaging: Transvaginal ultrasound or pelvic CT scan

easy.intro: Initial History Reason(s) for visit: Pelvic pain History of present Illness: This is a 20-year-old previously healthy female presenting to your office complaining of pelvic pain for the last month. She rates the pain as a constant 7/10 sharp pain which worsens when she moves. She was encouraged by her boyfriend to come to you because she is having extreme pain during sex. Ibuprofen "takes the edge off." The pain is severe enough that she reports having difficulty sleeping. Her pain worsens during her menstruation and that the pain is "unbearable." She admits that she has had unprotected sex with her boyfriend a few times, but reports that she is taking an oral contraceptive. She denies multiple sexual partners in the past. She has had a few episodes of vomiting and still feels nauseous. No fevers or chills. She also reports pain when having a bowel movement. She denies any vaginal discharge. Past Medical History: Current medications: Oral Contraceptive Allergies: None Vaccinations: Received Childhood vaccinations. Women's health: She has never been to a gynecologist. Family History: The patient's mother has a history of breast cancer in her 50s. Her father had a 'heart attack' in his 40s. Marital/Family: The patient is the oldest of 3 children. Both of her siblings are healthy without medical problems. Personal habits: She admits to smoking 2 cigarettes a day to help lose weight. She also admits to smoking marijuana occasionally at parties. She also drinks alcohol once a week with friends. Occupation/educational: She is a college student and currently studying Art History and would like to be a teacher. Recreational: She enjoys to paint and train dogs. interventions.label: Obtain medical history including menstrual cycle, sexual activity, contraceptive use, and any known bleeding disorders

interventions.label: Complete blood count (CBC) including hemoglobin, hematocrit, and mean corpuscular volume (MCV)
interventions.why: To evaluate for anemia as a potential cause of fatigue. MCV is particularly useful in determining the type of anemia.

interventions.label: Metabolic panel including glucose, electrolytes, liver function tests, and creatinine
interventions.why: To assess for metabolic abnormalities that may contribute to symptoms of fatigue.

interventions.label: Oxygen
interventions.why: The patient presents with fatigue, which can be a symptom of various conditions such as anemia, infection, or metabolic disorders. However, fatigue is also a common symptom that may indicate respiratory distress, making oxygen administration necessary to ensure adequate oxygenation.

interventions.label: IV access ×2
interventions.why: Intravenous access is crucial for administering fluids, medications, and monitoring the patient's vital signs.

interventions.label: Obtain a detailed history, including description of the assault, any injuries, and risk for STIs or pregnancy.
Perform a comprehensive physical exam focusing on genitourinary and rectal examination.
Collect forensic evidence (rape kit) if appropriate and with patient's consent.
Order laboratory tests: hepatitis B and C serology, HIV test, RPR for syphilis, gonorrhea and chlamydia cultures/nucleic acid amplification testing (NAAT).
Consult Sexual Assault Forensic Examiner (SAFE) team or Sexual Assault Nurse Examiner (SANE) for comprehensive care and support.
Provide psychosocial support, including referral to mental health services.
Oxygen
interventions.why: The patient is a sexual assault victim, and it's important to provide immediate oxygen to maintain oxygenation. Also, the patient might be in distress or have symptoms of AMS.

interventions.label: IV access ×2 large-bore
interventions.why: It is essential to establish two large-bore IV access lines for fluid resuscitation and potential medication administration.
