interventions.label: Obtain vital signs
interventions.why: To assess the patient's overall condition

interventions.label: Perform physical examination with focus on abdominal examination
interventions.why: To identify potential localizing signs for abdominal pain

interventions.label: Order complete blood count (CBC), basic metabolic panel, lipase, amylase
interventions.why: For general diagnostic workup of acute abdomen

interventions.label: Consider imaging studies such as ultrasound or CT scan of the abdomen
interventions.why: To help determine the cause of the abdominal pain and identify any potential complications

interventions.label: Consult general surgery if signs of surgical emergency (e.g., peritonitis, rebound tenderness)
interventions.why: For management of potential surgical emergencies

interventions.label: Initiate broad-spectrum antibiotics for suspected intra-abdominal infection
interventions.why: To cover common abdominal pathogens while waiting for culture results

interventions.label: Consider pain management as needed
interventions.why: For comfort and to help manage the patient's symptoms

interventions.label: Oxygen
interventions.why: Abdominal pain could be indicative of a variety of conditions, some potentially life-threatening. Oxygen is a necessary first step to ensure adequate oxygenation and stabilization.

interventions.label: Order: Order: time-sensitive interventions first; (oxygen, IV access, glucose check)
interventions.why: IV access is crucial for administering fluids, medications, and blood products if needed. A glucose check can help rule out diabetes as a potential cause of the abdominal pain.

easy.intro: Initial History Reason(s) for visit: Abdominal Pain History of present Illness: A 22-year-old female presents to the emergency room with abdominal pain, lethargy, and vomiting x2 days. Her vomiting is non-bloody. Her abdominal pain is diffuse, which she rates as a 6/10. She also claims to have had an increased appetite prior to having these symptoms. She also claims that she’s been developing blurry vision, dizziness, and drowsiness. She also states that she is having increased urination Past Medical History Past Medical History: None Current medications: Birth control Allergies: None Vaccinations: The patient received her childhood vaccinations. She has never received any HPV vaccines. Women's health: The patient cannot remember when her last pap smear was done. Family History: The patient has a brother with Diabetes. Social History: Marital/Family: Single. She is an undergraduate student at a local university. Personal habits: The patient denies smoking. She drinks socially. She smoked marijuana "a few times" with her friends. She denies other illicit drugs or misuse of medications. 180.title: Headache
180.vitalsText: Initial Vital Signs Day 1 @ 9:00 Temperature: 38.3 C (101.0 degrees F) Pulse: 88 beats/min. Regular rhythm Respiratory rate: 18 /minute Blood pressure, systolic: 118 mm Hg Blood pressure, diastolic: 70 mm Hg Height: 175.3 cm (69.0 in) Weight: 65.8 kg (145 lb) Body Mass Index: 21.4 kg/m2
exam: presents to your office with a an occipital headache, mild vertigo, and a tendency to fall when walking for 6 days.
Abdominal tenderness pattern matches history; no rigid abdomen documented yet.
Mental status intact; cranial nerves and neck stiffness assessed.
Skin rash morphology and distribution documented.
180.clinical_tip: Thunderclap headache = SAH workup. Do not send home without considering red flags.
180.objective: Rule out SAH, meningitis, mass, and hypertensive emergency.

interventions.label: Obtain a detailed history and perform a physical examination
Order relevant laboratory investigations (e.g., complete blood count, electrolytes, glucose, thyroid function tests, and lumbar puncture if necessary)
Request imaging studies such as CT or MRI of the brain
Consult with a neurologist for further evaluation and management
If the patient's condition is stable, consider admitting the patient for observation
Oxygen
interventions.why: The case presents with a headache, which could be indicative of various conditions, some of which may require immediate oxygen therapy.

interventions.label: IV access ×2
interventions.why: In any medical emergency, establishing two large-bore IV access is crucial for administration of fluids and medications.

easy.intro: Initial History Reason(s) for visit: Headache History of present Illness: A 55-year-old female with a past medical history of hypertension presents to your office with a an occipital headache, mild vertigo, and a tendency to fall when walking for 6 days. She states that she never had any problems walking or being off-balance. Eight days ago, she stated that she had a pink rash on her arms and torso and felt feverish. The rash improved after a couple days, but the headache and tendency to fall persisted. She had nausea, but she denied any vomiting. She feels weak and "under the weather." She denies travel, but went hiking 2 weeks ago nearby her house which she routinely does every summer. She denies any history of migraines. She will occasionally get a tension headache which she will treat with Ibuprofen. She has never had a headache that has lasted this long. She denies any trauma to her head. She denies any sick contacts. She denies any recent antibiotic use. She is very concerned about her symptoms. PMH: Past Medical History: Hypertension. The patient recently had a colonoscopy screening which was normal. Current medications: HCTZ 25mg PO daily Allergies: None Vaccinations: Received Childhood vaccinations. Women's health: The patient cannot remember when her last pap smear was done. Family History: Her father has a history of Hypertension and died of a stroke. Her mother has no health problems and is still living. Social History: Marital/Family: She is married with one child. She lives in Gaithersburg, Maryland. Personal habits: The patient denies smoking. She lives an active lifestyle. She drinks alcohol socially. She denies use of illegal drugs or misuse of medications. Occupation/educational: She works as a public relations specialist. She is a college graduate with a BS in communication. Recreational: The patient enjoys to bike and hike. She loves the outdoors and spends as much time as she is able. Review of systems: General: See HPI Skin: See HPI HEENT: Negative Cardiorespiratory: Negative Gastrointestinal: See HPI Genitourinary: Negative Neuropsychiatric: See HPI --- Chart tabs --- * Chart: Progress Notes → presentation_5_Headache_chart_Progress_Notes.png * Chart: Vital Signs → presentation_5_Headache_chart_Vital_Signs.png

181.title: Nocturnal Enuresis
181.diagnosis: The most likely diagnoses for Nocturnal Enuresis in an adult are psychiatric conditions (e.g., anxiety, depression), neurological disorders, or drug/substance use. However, it's important to note that the diagnosis is still unknown in this case.
exam: Nocturnal Enuresis — exam tailored to chief complaint on arrival.
Soft, non-distended, no focal peritoneal signs on initial exam.
Alert and oriented unless perfusion or metabolic derangement present.
Warm skin with fever; no purpura on initial survey.
181.clinical_tip: Febrile infant — sepsis workup, fluids, and close monitoring before disposition.
181.objective: Stabilize the pediatric patient and complete emergent workup before disposition.

interventions.label: Obtain a thorough history and physical examination
interventions.why: This is the first step in any clinical scenario to gather relevant information about the patient's symptoms, past medical history, and other potential factors contributing to the presentation.

interventions.label: Urinalysis (to rule out underlying UTI or other renal issues)
interventions.why: A urinalysis can help identify possible causes such as a urinary tract infection or other kidney-related conditions that may be associated with Nocturnal Enuresis.

interventions.label: Oxygen
interventions.why: Nocturnal Enuresis is not a life-threatening condition, but the case does not provide information about the patient's condition upon presentation. Therefore, it's essential to begin with lifesaving interventions that could address potential underlying issues.

interventions.label: IV access ×2
interventions.why: Intravenous access is needed for various tests and treatments during hospitalization.

interventions.label: Monitor
interventions.why: Vital sign monitoring is crucial in any patient encounter to assess the overall condition and response to treatment.

interventions.label: Pulse ox
interventions.why: Oxygen saturation measurement is an essential initial step to evaluate a patient's respiratory status.

easy.objective: Stabilize the pediatric patient and complete emergent workup before disposition. Start with stabilization, then targeted testing.

001.title: Chest Pain
001.vitalsText: Initial Vital Signs Day 1 @ 16:00 Temperature: 37.0 C (98.6 degrees F) Pulse: 120 beats/min, Weak Respiratory rate: 34 /minute Blood pressure, systolic: 100 mm Hg Blood pressure, diastolic: 60 mm Hg Height: 183cm (72.0 in) Weight: 97.5 kg (215 lb) Body Mass Index: 29.1 kg/m2
exam: Tachycardic, regular rhythm, no new murmur
001.clinical_tip: Needle decompression before CXR in the unstable patient — do not delay treatment for imaging.
001.objective: Immediate decompression and chest tube; 100% oxygen.

interventions.label: Move to Emergency Department
interventions.why: Acute respiratory failure requires ED care.

interventions.label: IV access
interventions.why: Large-bore access if hypotensive or peri-arrest.

interventions.label: Needle decompression
interventions.why: Clinical diagnosis — treat before imaging if unstable.

decoys.label: Administer a sedative
decoys.why: Sedatives are not typically administered initially in tension pneumothorax as they can worsen respiratory depression

decoys.label: Start antibiotics
decoys.why: Antibiotics are not necessary for a primary diagnosis of tension pneumothorax unless there is a secondary infection present

decoys.label: Apply a hot pack to the affected area
decoys.why: Heat application is not beneficial in cases of tension pneumothorax as it can increase intrathoracic pressure and worsen symptoms

decoys.label: Prescribe a blood transfusion
decoys.why: A blood transfusion would not be appropriate for a primary diagnosis of tension pneumothorax unless there is severe anemia or hemorrhage present

easy.intro: Initial History Reason(s) for visit: Chest pain; respiratory distress History of present Illness: The patient, a 65-year-old accountant, is brought to the emergency department by ambulance from the trucking company, where he works. Ten minutes prior to the ambulance’s arrival, the patient developed excruciating, sharp pain on the right side of his chest accompanied by marked respiratory distress. He rates the pain as an 8 on a 10-point scale. The pain increases on respiration, and he is unable to answer questions. A coworker who accompanied the patient to the hospital says that this has never happened to the patient in the past. Coworker mentions patient has a past medical history of emphysema and asthma. Oxygen was administered during transport.

002.title: Altered Mental Status
002.vitalsText: Initial Vital Signs Day 1 @ 11:00 Temperature: 36.8 C (98.2 degrees F) Pulse: 88 beats/min. Regular rhythm Respiratory rate: 18 /minute Blood pressure, systolic: 115 mm Hg Blood pressure, diastolic: 85 mm Hg Height: 180cm (70.9 in) Weight: 91.0 kg (200.6 lb) Body Mass Index: 27.9 kg/m2
exam: Confused, follows simple commands; gait unsteady after recent fall
002.clinical_tip: Elderly or alcoholic with AMS after minor trauma — non-contrast CT head first.
002.objective: Identify bleed, reverse coagulopathy, neurosurgical evacuation if indicated.

interventions.label: Emergency Department
interventions.why: Acute neurologic change needs ED evaluation.

interventions.label: Seizure precautions
interventions.why: Prevent secondary brain injury from seizures.

interventions.label: Neurosurgery consult
interventions.why: Evacuation if midline shift or decline.

decoys.label: Admit the patient to a general ward
decoys.why: Subdural hematoma is an emergency and requires immediate attention. Admission to a general ward might not provide the necessary level of care, monitoring, or resources for such a critical condition.

decoys.label: Prescribe pain medication but without any CT scan
decoys.why: Pain management is important, but it should be done after confirming the cause of the head injury. Without a CT scan, the cause cannot be accurately identified, and the wrong treatment might be prescribed.

decoys.label: Administer a blood thinner to prevent further clotting
decoys.why: Subdural hematoma is caused by bleeding between the brain and skull. Administering a blood thinner could potentially worsen the condition by accelerating the bleeding.

decoys.label: Start the patient on antiepileptic drugs for seizure prophylaxis without a history of seizures
decoys.why: Antiepileptic drugs are used to prevent seizures, but they should only be administered if the patient has a history of seizures or if seizures have occurred during the initial evaluation. In the absence of these factors, their use is unnecessary and potentially harmful.

easy.intro: Initial History Reason(s) for visit: Altered mental status History of present Illness: A 70-year-old Caucasian man comes to the Emergency room because his family members are concerned that something is wrong. The patient has been having behavioral changes over a period of three or four weeks. He used to be very active and managed all of his financial affairs. He now barely talks, stares at the wall for hours at a time, and sleeps most of the day. Four weeks ago, the patient got up to urinate and fell before reaching the bathroom. He was found on the floor the next morning by family members. He was taken to his family doctor and was discharged with a prescription to buy a cane. Since that time, he has been having problems walking. There have been no fevers or night sweats. The patient is currently in no pain. Past Medical History: Chronic alcohol abuse; cataracts. Hospitalization Procedures: Hemorrhoidectomy 1 year ago. Last colonoscopy was 4 years ago which demonstrated no abnormalities. Current medications: Aspirin 81mg PO daily Allergies: None Vaccinations: Up to date Family History: Father: Deceased due to a 'heart attack'. Mother: Diagnosed with type 2 diabetes mellitus and died of natural causes. The patient has no siblings. Social History: Marital/Family: Widowed; he has three children. Personal habits: the patient smokes 1 Pack/day. He currently does not drink alcohol but was a chronic alcoholic for 30 years. Denies use of illegal drugs or misuse of medications. Occupation/educational: He is a retired Army human resources manager. He is a high school graduate. Recreational: The patient likes to spend time with his children and enjoys collecting stamps. 003.title: Pelvic Pain
003.vitalsText: Initial Vital Signs Day 1 @ 9:00 Temperature: 37.0 C (98.6 degrees F) Pulse: 110 beat/min, Tachycardic Respiratory rate: 22 /minute Blood pressure, systolic: 140 mm Hg Blood pressure, diastolic: 92 mm Hg Height: 175.3 cm (69.0 in) Weight: 65.8 kg (145 lb) Body Mass Index: 21.4 kg/m2
exam: Suprapubic and unilateral lower quadrant tenderness
003.clinical_tip: Positive pregnancy test changes everything — ectopic until proven otherwise.
003.objective: Rule out ectopic, torsion, and rupture.

interventions.why: Need to rule out other causes of pain (e.g. renal colic, appendagitis) before considering testosterone.
Imaging is not recommended in the initial management of suspected constipation.
Given the history of renal colic, renal imaging should have been considered first to rule out an obstruction or nephrolithiasis.
It is not appropriate to perform a testosterone level in the initial management of suspected constipation without addressing underlying causes such as renal colic, appendagitis, etc.

004.title: Abdominal Pain
004.vitalsText: Initial Vital Signs Day 1 @ 18:00 Temperature: 37.2 C (98.9 degrees F) Pulse: 106 beat/min, Tachycardic Respiratory rate: 20 /minute Blood pressure, systolic: 114 mm Hg Blood pressure, diastolic: 79 mm Hg Height: 160.0 cm (63.0 in) Weight: 80.5 kg (177.4 lb) Body Mass Index: 31.4 kg/m2
exam: presents to the emergency room with abdominal pain, lethargy, and vomiting x2 days.
Abdominal tenderness pattern matches history; no rigid abdomen documented yet.
Alert and oriented unless perfusion or metabolic derangement present.
No acute rash; capillary refill and perfusion assessed.
004.clinical_tip: Elderly and immunocompromised may have minimal exam findings until rupture.
004.objective: Resuscitate, image, involve surgery early if indicated.

easy.intro: Initial History Reason(s) for visit: Abdominal Pain History of present Illness: A 22-year-old female presents to the emergency room with abdominal pain, lethargy, and vomiting x2 days. Her vomiting is non-bloody. Her abdominal pain is diffuse, which she rates as a 6/10. She also claims to have had an increased appetite prior to having these symptoms. She also claims that she’s been developing blurry vision, dizziness, and drowsiness. She also states that she is having increased urination Past Medical History Past Medical History: None Current medications: Birth control Allergies: None Vaccinations: The patient received her childhood vaccinations. She has never received any HPV vaccines. Women's health: The patient cannot remember when her last pap smear was done. Family History: The patient has a brother with Diabetes. Social History: Marital/Family: Single. She is an undergraduate student at a local university. Personal habits: The patient denies smoking. She drinks socially. She smoked marijuana "a few times" with her friends. She denies other illicit drugs or misuse of medications. Occupation/educational: She is a student at a local university Recreational: She plays the piano and sings in a band. Review of systems: General: See HPI Skin: Her skin is dry. 005.title: Headache
005.vitalsText: Initial Vital Signs Day 1 @ 9:00 Temperature: 38.3 C (101.0 degrees F) Pulse: 88 beats/min. Regular rhythm Respiratory rate: 18 /minute Blood pressure, systolic: 118 mm Hg Blood pressure, diastolic: 70 mm Hg Height: 175.3 cm (69.0 in) Weight: 65.8 kg (145 lb) Body Mass Index: 21.4 kg/m2
exam: presents to your office with a an occipital headache, mild vertigo, and a tendency to fall when walking for 6 days.
Abdominal tenderness pattern matches history; no rigid abdomen documented yet.
Mental status intact; cranial nerves and neck stiffness assessed.
Skin rash morphology and distribution documented.
005.clinical_tip: Thunderclap headache = SAH workup. Do not send home without considering red flags.
005.objective: Rule out SAH, meningitis, mass, and hypertensive emergency.

006.title: Rash and Lethargy
exam: Soft, may have mild tenderness if systemic illness
Lethargy with intact or fluctuating mental status
Rash distribution and morphology documented; petechiae ruled out if toxic
006.clinical_tip: Petechiae + fever in a sick patient — treat empirically for meningococcemia.
006.objective: Recognize toxic rash syndromes. Resuscitate and cover infection.

007.title: Generalized Weakness
007.vitalsText: Initial Vital Signs Day 1 @ 9:00 Temperature: 37.0 C (98.6 degrees F) Pulse: 110 beat/min, Regular rhythm; bounding Respiratory rate: 18 /minute Blood pressure, systolic: 122 mm Hg Blood pressure, diastolic: 74 mm Hg Height: 175.3 cm (69.0 in) Weight: 65.8 kg (145 lb) Body Mass Index: 21.4 kg/m2
exam: presents to your office complaining of progressive generalized weakness that has been occurring for the past 3 months.
Soft, non-distended, no focal peritoneal signs on initial exam.
Motor strength and reflexes tested for fatigability and focal deficits.
Skin rash morphology and distribution documented.
007.clinical_tip: Weakness + hyperreflexia vs hyporeflexia localizes — think electrolytes and cord.
007.objective: Differentiate metabolic, neurologic, and toxic causes.

interventions.label: MRI Spine if cord signs
interventions.why: Cord compression is a surgical emergency.

interventions.label: Admit / Neuro consult
interventions.why: Progressive weakness or respiratory concern → ICU.

easy.intro: Initial History Reason(s) for visit: Generalized weakness History of present Illness: A previously healthy 30-year-old female with a PMH of asthma presents to your office complaining of progressive generalized weakness that has been occurring for the past 3 months. Her symptoms worsen at the end of the day, and her weakness improves with rest. She reports occasional blurry vision with improvement after closing and resting her eyes. She also notes difficulty chewing food and swallowing recently. She has also been experiencing dyspnea with strenuous exercise, but it is now refractory to her albuterol inhaler. Past Medical History: She broke her arm when she was 14 which required an open reduction and pin placement. Current medications: Birth control, multi-vitamin Allergies: Penicillin (rash and shortness of breath) Vaccinations: Received Childhood vaccinations. Women's health: She contracted gonorrhea from a sexual partner at age 20 which was successfully treated. She had a LEEP procedure done due to an abnormal pap smear 6 years ago. Her last pap smear was done 3 years ago which was normal. Family History: Her father has a history of congestive heart failure and is status post two myocardial infarctions. Her mother has hypothyroidism. Her grandfather died of a stroke. Social History: Marital/Family: The patient is single. She is currently living and is sexually active with her boyfriend of 2 years. Personal habits: She states that she drinks occasionally. The patient smokes cigars with her boyfriend on occasion when going out. Occupation/educational: She works as a newspaper editor for a local newspaper. Recreational: She enjoys reading and breeds dogs. Review of systems: General: See HPI Skin: Negative HEENT: See HPI Cardiorespiratory: Negative Gastrointestinal: Negative Genitourinary: Negative Neuropsychiatric: See HPI

easy.intro: Initial History Reason(s) for visit: Burning during urination History of present Illness: The patient, a 19 year old college freshman, presents to your office stating that he has a 5/10, sharp pain when he urinates. The pain stops when urination is complete. The pain began 3 days ago and has been progressively getting worse. He also recently developed testicular pain and pain with sex, especially with ejaculation. He is sexually active with his girlfriend of 6 months, and although they typically use condoms, they occasionally have unprotected sex. He also confirms chills, lethargy, a subjective fever, decreased appetite, but denies nausea and vomiting. Tylenol has improved his pain and fever. Past Medical History: Fractured ankle. Current medications: Tylenol 500mg as needed Allergies: None Vaccinations: He received his childhood vaccinations. Family History: The patient's father has hypertension. His mother has hypothyroidism. Social History: The patient is a college freshman, and had his first sexual encounter at age 15. He states that he never had a sexually transmitted infection before. He is a social drinker and has experimented with marijuana in the past. He denies any other illicit drug use. He enjoys rock climbing. Review of systems: General: See HPI Skin: Negative HEENT: Negative Cardiorespiratory: Negative Gastrointestinal: Negative Genitourinary: See HPI Neuropsychiatric: Negative

easy.intro: Initial History Reason(s) for visit: Abdominal Pain History of present Illness: A 22-year-old female presents to the emergency room with abdominal pain, lethargy, and vomiting x2 days. Her vomiting is non-bloody. Her abdominal pain is diffuse, which she rates as a 6/10. She also claims to have had an increased appetite prior to having these symptoms. She also claims that she’s been developing blurry vision, dizziness, and drowsiness. She also states that she is having increased urination Past Medical History Past Medical History: None Current medications: Birth control Allergies: None Vaccinations: The patient received her childhood vaccinations. She has never received any HPV vaccines. Women's health: The patient cannot remember when her last pap smear was done. Family History: The patient has a brother with Diabetes. Social History: Marital/Family: Single. She is an undergraduate student at a local university. Personal habits: The patient denies smoking. She drinks socially. 010.title: Pain in Legs
exam: Pain in Legs — exam tailored to chief complaint on arrival.
Soft, non-distended, no focal peritoneal signs on initial exam.
Alert and oriented unless perfusion or metabolic derangement present.
No acute rash; capillary refill and perfusion assessed.
010.clinical_tip: Stabilize first — ABCs, IV access, monitor, then targeted workup.
010.objective: Identify and treat life threats within the time limit.

interventions.label: A
interventions.why: Brown infections on the dermis, likely due to a systemic issue such as diabetes. Topical treatments like creams or ointments may not be effective; systemic antibiotics may be required.

interventions.label: D
interventions.why: Cases of brown lesions in the genital area, especially if accompanied by pruritus and discomfort, could indicate an STI such as chlamydia or gonorrhea. Treatment would depend on test results but often involves antibiotics.

interventions.label: B
interventions.why: The diagnosis is not supported by the image provided. The patient's history, physical examination, and current treatment regimen do not suggest diabetes as the cause of the skin lesions.

interventions.label: C
interventions.why: Given the presence of brown lesions in the genital area and associated symptoms, chlamydia or gonorrhea are more likely causes. Swabs for STIs would be an appropriate initial diagnostic test.

011.title: Abdominal Pain
011.vitalsText: Initial Vital Signs Day 1 @ 18:00 Temperature: 37.2 C (98.9 degrees F) Pulse: 106 beat/min, Tachycardic Respiratory rate: 20 /minute Blood pressure, systolic: 114 mm Hg Blood pressure, diastolic: 79 mm Hg Height: 160.0 cm (63.0 in) Weight: 80.5 kg (177.4 lb) Body Mass Index: 31.4 kg/m2
exam: presents to the emergency room with abdominal pain, lethargy, and vomiting x2 days.
Abdominal tenderness pattern matches history; no rigid abdomen documented yet.
Alert and oriented unless perfusion or metabolic derangement present.
No acute rash; capillary refill and perfusion assessed.
011.clinical_tip: Elderly and immunocompromised may have minimal exam findings until rupture.
011.objective: Resuscitate, image, involve surgery early if indicated.

interventions.label: Perform complete physical examination, with special focus on abdominal exam
Order laboratory tests: Complete Blood Count (CBC), Electrolytes, Liver Function Tests (LFTs), Amylase, Lipase
Order imaging studies: Abdominal ultrasound, CT scan (consider IV contrast)
Consult Surgery for possible exploratory laparotomy if findings are suggestive of acute surgical abdomen
Oxygen
interventions.why: The case presents with abdominal pain which could be life-threatening. The patient may be experiencing distress or AMS, hence immediate oxygen is necessary.

interventions.label: Order: Order: time-sensitive interventions first; (oxygen, IV access, glucose check)
interventions.why: IV access is essential for administering fluids and medications. Glucose check is important as hypoglycemia can present with abdominal pain.

012.title: Burning During Urination
012.diagnosis: Possible causes include Urinary Tract Infection (UTI), Genital Herpes, STDs, or Kidney Stones.
012.vitalsText: Initial Vital Signs Day 1 @ 9:00 Temperature: 38.3 C (101.0 degrees F) Pulse: 89 beat/min, Regular rhythm Respiratory rate: 20 /minute Blood pressure, systolic: 122 mm Hg Blood pressure, diastolic: 74 mm Hg Height: 183cm (72.0 in) Weight: 97.5 kg (215 lb) Body Mass Index: 29.1 kg/m2
exam: presents to your office stating that he has a 5/10, sharp pain when he urinates.
Abdominal tenderness pattern matches history; no rigid abdomen documented yet.
Alert and oriented unless perfusion or metabolic derangement present.
No acute rash; capillary refill and perfusion assessed.
012.clinical_tip: Complicated UTI in men, pregnancy, or fever → admit and image.
012.objective: Treat infection; rule out pyelonephritis and STI.

interventions.label: Obtain a urinalysis and urine culture
interventions.why: These tests help identify the presence of bacteria, indicating a UTI. They also allow for antibiotic sensitivity testing.

interventions.label: Oxygen
interventions.why: The patient is experiencing burning during urination, a potential symptom of a urinary tract infection (UTI). However, the symptoms could also indicate other serious conditions such as kidney stones or a sexually transmitted disease. To stabilize the patient immediately, it's crucial to ensure adequate oxygenation and establish IV access for fluid resuscitation.

interventions.label: IV access ×2 large-bore
interventions.why: Large-bore IV access is necessary to allow rapid administration of fluids in case of hypovolemia or sepsis, both potential complications of UTI.

easy.intro: Initial History Reason(s) for visit: Burning during urination History of present Illness: The patient, a 19 year old college freshman, presents to your office stating that he has a 5/10, sharp pain when he urinates. The pain stops when urination is complete. The pain began 3 days ago and has been progressively getting worse. He also recently developed testicular pain and pain with sex, especially with ejaculation. He is sexually active with his girlfriend of 6 months, and although they typically use condoms, they occasionally have unprotected sex. He also confirms chills, lethargy, a subjective fever, decreased appetite, but denies nausea and vomiting. Tylenol has improved his pain and fever. Past Medical History: Fractured ankle. Current medications: Tylenol 500mg as needed Allergies: None Vaccinations: He received his childhood vaccinations. Family History: The patient's father has hypertension. His mother has hypothyroidism. Social History: The patient is a college freshman, and had his first sexual encounter at age 15. He states that he never had a sexually transmitted infection before. He is a social drinker and has experimented with marijuana in the past. He denies any other illicit drug use. He enjoys rock climbing. Review of systems: General: See HPI Skin: Negative HEENT: Negative Cardiorespiratory: Negative Gastrointestinal: Negative Genitourinary: See HPI Neuropsychiatric: Negative

013.title: Abdominal Pain
013.vitalsText: Initial Vital Signs Day 1 @ 18:00 Temperature: 37.2 C (98.9 degrees F) Pulse: 106 beat/min, Tachycardic Respiratory rate: 20 /minute Blood pressure, systolic: 114 mm Hg Blood pressure, diastolic: 79 mm Hg Height: 160.0 cm (63.0 in) Weight: 80.5 kg (177.4 lb) Body Mass Index: 31.4 kg/m2
exam: presents to the emergency room with abdominal pain, lethargy, and vomiting x2 days.
Abdominal tenderness pattern matches history; no rigid abdomen documented yet.
Alert and oriented unless perfusion or metabolic derangement present.
No acute rash; capillary refill and perfusion assessed.
013.clinical_tip: Elderly and immunocompromised may have minimal exam findings until rupture.
013.objective: Resuscitate, image, involve surgery early if indicated.

interventions.label: Obtain laboratory tests: CBC, metabolic panel, lipase
Obtain imaging studies: CT abdomen/pelvis with contrast (if renal function is normal), ultrasound if contraindicated for CT
Consult general surgery for possible exploratory laparotomy or other interventions as needed
Oxygen
interventions.why: To ensure adequate oxygenation in cases of abdominal pain which can be a sign of various emergencies

interventions.label: Order: Order: time-sensitive interventions first; (oxygen, IV access, glucose check)
interventions.why: To provide immediate stabilization and start critical investigations

014.title: Facial and Upper Extremity Edema
014.diagnosis: Lymphedema or venous congestion (edematous disorders), Deep vein thrombosis (DVT) or Pulmonary embolism (PE) as differential diagnoses
exam: Facial and Upper Extremity Edema — exam tailored to chief complaint on arrival.
Soft, non-distended, no focal peritoneal signs on initial exam.
Alert and oriented unless perfusion or metabolic derangement present.
No acute rash; capillary refill and perfusion assessed.
014.clinical_tip: Stabilize first — ABCs, IV access, monitor, then targeted workup.
014.objective: Identify and treat life threats within the time limit.

interventions.label: Obtain a complete blood count (CBC)
interventions.why: To evaluate for anemia, infection, or other systemic conditions that may be causing the edema

interventions.label: Perform D-dimer test
interventions.why: As a quick and non-invasive screening test for DVT/PE, positive results warrant further imaging investigation

interventions.label: Obtain duplex ultrasound of the lower extremities for DVT evaluation
interventions.why: To confirm or rule out DVT if clinical suspicion is high or D-dimer test is positive

interventions.label: Consult with a vascular surgeon or internist for further assessment and management
Initiate supportive care: elevation, compression bandages, and diuretics as needed
Oxygen
interventions.why: The patient is exhibiting facial and upper extremity edema which may indicate severe distress or acute medical emergency. Immediate administration of oxygen is essential to maintain adequate oxygenation.

interventions.label: IV access ×2
interventions.why: Large-bore IV access is necessary for prompt delivery of fluids, medications, and blood products if needed in this critical situation.

interventions.label: Glucose check
interventions.why: Hypoglycemia can cause symptoms similar to those described in the case. A quick glucose check can help determine if the patient's condition is worsened by low blood sugar levels.

015.title: Chest Pain
015.diagnosis: Acute Coronary Syndrome (ACS) or other cardiac causes are most likely considering the chief complaint of Chest Pain.
015.vitalsText: Initial Vital Signs Day 1 @ 16:00 Temperature: 37.0 C (98.6 degrees F) Pulse: 120 beats/min, Weak Respiratory rate: 34 /minute Blood pressure, systolic: 100 mm Hg Blood pressure, diastolic: 60 mm Hg Height: 183cm (72.0 in) Weight: 97.5 kg (215 lb) Body Mass Index: 29.1 kg/m2
exam: Chest Pain — exam tailored to chief complaint on arrival.
Soft, non-distended, no focal peritoneal signs on initial exam.
Alert and oriented unless perfusion or metabolic derangement present.
No acute rash; capillary refill and perfusion assessed.
015.clinical_tip: Until proven otherwise, treat unstable chest pain as ACS.
015.objective: Rule out life threats. ECG within 10 minutes.

interventions.label: EKG - to evaluate for signs of acute ischemia
Troponin labs - to assess for myocardial damage
Oxygen therapy if patient is dyspneic or hypoxic
Morphine for analgesia and potential vasodilation
ASA (acetylsalicylic acid) - to reduce the risk of thrombosis
Beta-blockers (if not contraindicated) - for rate control and reduction in oxygen demand
Consult Cardiology or Cardiac Care Unit (CCU) - for further management and potential interventions such as PCI or CABG
monitor
interventions.why: To keep track of the patient's vital signs

interventions.label: EKG
interventions.why: To evaluate for possible ischemia or arrhythmias

interventions.label: troponins x serial
interventions.why: To monitor for myocardial injury and diagnose NSTEMI/ STEMI

easy.intro: Initial History Reason(s) for visit: Chest pain; respiratory distress History of present Illness: The patient, a 65-year-old accountant, is brought to the emergency department by ambulance from the trucking company, where he works. Ten minutes prior to the ambulance’s arrival, the patient developed excruciating, sharp pain on the right side of his chest accompanied by marked respiratory distress. He rates the pain as an 8 on a 10-point scale. The pain increases on respiration, and he is unable to answer questions. A coworker who accompanied the patient to the hospital says that this has never happened to the patient in the past. Coworker mentions patient has a past medical history of emphysema and asthma. Oxygen was administered during transport.

interventions.label: Place the patient: in the appropriate location (ER → ICU vs floor)
interventions.why: Depending on the severity and potential urgency of the patient's condition, they may require immediate transfer to an intensive care unit. Initial placement in an appropriate location allows for timely management and monitoring.

easy.intro: Initial History Reason(s) for visit: Abdominal Pain History of present Illness: A 22-year-old female presents to the emergency room with abdominal pain, lethargy, and vomiting x2 days. Her vomiting is non-bloody. Her abdominal pain is diffuse, which she rates as a 6/10. She also claims to have had an increased appetite prior to having these symptoms. She also claims that she’s been developing blurry vision, dizziness, and drowsiness. She also states that she is having increased urination Past Medical History Past Medical History: None Current medications: Birth control Allergies: None Vaccinations: The patient received her childhood vaccinations. She has never received any HPV vaccines. Women's health: The patient cannot remember when her last pap smear was done. Family History: The patient has a brother with Diabetes. Social History: Marital/Family: Single. She is an undergraduate student at a local university. Personal habits: The patient denies smoking. She drinks socially. She smoked marijuana "a few times" with her friends. She denies other illicit drugs or misuse of medications. Occupation/educational: She is a student at a local university Recreational: She plays the piano and sings in a band. Review of systems: General: See HPI Skin: Her skin is dry. HEENT: Her throat is dry. Cardiorespiratory: She feels that her heart is beating faster. 022.title: Found Unconscious
022.diagnosis: Acute Brain Injury (ABI) or Cardiopulmonary Arrest
exam: Unresponsive on arrival, airway and circulation addressed first
Deferred until stable unless trauma pathway
GCS documented; pupils and focal signs assessed
Trauma survey for lacerations, track marks, temperature
022.clinical_tip: Fingerstick glucose before CT — hypoglycemia mimics stroke.

interventions.label: Vital signs and airway assessment
interventions.why: Assess patient's vital signs and airway status to determine the urgency of intervention

interventions.label: Order blood glucose and basic metabolic panel
interventions.why: Hypoglycemia can cause altered mental status. Basic metabolic panel aids in identifying electrolyte imbalances that may have contributed to the patient's condition

interventions.label: Send STAT head CT to evaluate for intracranial hemorrhage or trauma
Administer high-flow oxygen
interventions.why: High-flow oxygen supplementation can help maintain adequate oxygenation in patients with unknown cause of unconsciousness

interventions.label: Initiate Advanced Cardiac Life Support (ACLS) measures if necessary
Oxygen
interventions.why: The patient is unconscious, which could be due to lack of oxygen. Giving oxygen immediately can help prevent further complications.

interventions.label: IV access ×2
interventions.why: Intravenous access is essential for administering necessary fluids, medications, and blood products.

interventions.label: glucose check
interventions.why: Hypoglycemia or hyperglycemia can cause unconsciousness. Checking the glucose level can help determine the appropriate treatment.

easy.clinicalTip: Teaching hint: Fingerstick glucose before CT — hypoglycemia mimics stroke.
easy.objective: Stabilize ABCs. Identify reversible causes. Start with stabilization, then targeted testing.

023.title: Shortness of Breath
023.diagnosis: Acute Respiratory Distress Syndrome (ARDS) or Pulmonary Embolism (PE) are potential causes for shortness of breath in undiagnosed cases.
exam: Dyspneic, speaking in short phrases if severe
023.clinical_tip: ABG or pulse ox + ECG + CXR in the first minutes.
023.objective: Stabilize hypoxia. Differentiate pulmonary vs cardiac vs metabolic.

interventions.label: Laboratory tests: Arterial blood gas (ABG), complete blood count (CBC), coagulation panel, D-dimer
Imaging: Chest X-ray, Computed Tomography Pulmonary Angiogram (CTPA) or Ventilation/Perfusion Scan (V/Q scan)
CMP/BMP
interventions.why: This order includes electrolytes, renal function, and liver function tests which are essential for various respiratory and metabolic disorders.

interventions.label: UA
interventions.why: A urinalysis is useful in diagnosing several conditions that could cause shortness of breath, such as pulmonary edema, pneumonia, or kidney disease.

easy.clinicalTip: Teaching hint: ABG or pulse ox + ECG + CXR in the first minutes.
easy.objective: Stabilize hypoxia. Differentiate pulmonary vs cardiac vs metabolic. Start with stabilization, then targeted testing.

024.title: Burning During Urination
024.diagnosis: Cystitis or Urinary Tract Infection (UTI) is the most likely diagnosis for this chief complaint in a CCS exam.
024.vitalsText: Initial Vital Signs Day 1 @ 9:00 Temperature: 38.3 C (101.0 degrees F) Pulse: 89 beat/min, Regular rhythm Respiratory rate: 20 /minute Blood pressure, systolic: 122 mm Hg Blood pressure, diastolic: 74 mm Hg Height: 183cm (72.0 in) Weight: 97.5 kg (215 lb) Body Mass Index: 29.1 kg/m2
exam: presents to your office stating that he has a 5/10, sharp pain when he urinates.
Abdominal tenderness pattern matches history; no rigid abdomen documented yet.
Alert and oriented unless perfusion or metabolic derangement present.
No acute rash; capillary refill and perfusion assessed.
024.clinical_tip: Complicated UTI in men, pregnancy, or fever → admit and image.
024.objective: Treat infection; rule out pyelonephritis and STI.

interventions.label: Obtain urine sample for culture and sensitivity
interventions.why: To identify the causative organism and determine appropriate antibiotic therapy.

interventions.label: Prescribe broad-spectrum antibiotics pending culture results

easy.intro: Initial History Reason(s) for visit: Burning during urination History of present Illness: The patient, a 19 year old college freshman, presents to your office stating that he has a 5/10, sharp pain when he urinates. The pain stops when urination is complete. The pain began 3 days ago and has been progressively getting worse. He also recently developed testicular pain and pain with sex, especially with ejaculation. He is sexually active with his girlfriend of 6 months, and although they typically use condoms, they occasionally have unprotected sex. He also confirms chills, lethargy, a subjective fever, decreased appetite, but denies nausea and vomiting. Tylenol has improved his pain and fever. Past Medical History: Fractured ankle. Current medications: Tylenol 500mg as needed Allergies: None Vaccinations: He received his childhood vaccinations. Family History: The patient's father has hypertension. His mother has hypothyroidism. Social History: The patient is a college freshman, and had his first sexual encounter at age 15. He states that he never had a sexually transmitted infection before. He is a social drinker and has experimented with marijuana in the past. He denies any other illicit drug use. He enjoys rock climbing. Review of systems: General: See HPI Skin: Negative HEENT: Negative Cardiorespiratory: Negative Gastrointestinal: Negative Genitourinary: See HPI Neuropsychiatric: Negative

025.title: Generalized Pain
025.diagnosis: Non-specific generalized pain may suggest various underlying conditions such as musculoskeletal disorders, infection, neoplasm, autoimmune diseases, or metabolic disorders. Initial evaluation should aim to narrow the diagnosis.
exam: Generalized Pain — exam tailored to chief complaint on arrival.
Soft, non-distended, no focal peritoneal signs on initial exam.
Alert and oriented unless perfusion or metabolic derangement present.
No acute rash; capillary refill and perfusion assessed.
025.clinical_tip: Stabilize first — ABCs, IV access, monitor, then targeted workup.
025.objective: Identify and treat life threats within the time limit.

interventions.label: Obtain a detailed history, including onset, location, severity, duration, aggravating and alleviating factors, associated symptoms, past medical history, medications, and family history.
Perform a comprehensive physical examination focusing on the musculoskeletal system, neurological system, and general appearance, vital signs, and skin.

Order laboratory tests: Complete Blood Count (CBC), Erythrocyte Sedimentation Rate (ESR), C-reactive protein (CRP), Basic Metabolic Panel, Liver Function Tests, Rheumatoid Factor, Antinuclear Antibodies, and HIV screening.
Order imaging studies: X-rays, Magnetic Resonance Imaging (MRI) or Computed Tomography (CT) based on the findings from history, physical exam, and laboratory tests.
Consultations: refer to a rheumatologist, orthopedist, neurologist, or oncologist as needed, depending on the results of initial workup.
Oxygen
interventions.why: Generalized Pain could indicate a variety of life-threatening conditions. Oxygen is necessary to ensure adequate oxygenation.

interventions.label: IV access ×2
interventions.why: Intravenous access is essential for administering medications and fluids, which may be required based on the patient's condition.

easy.clinicalTip: Teaching hint: Stabilize first — ABCs, IV access, monitor, then targeted workup.

026.title: Shortness of Breath
026.diagnosis: Acute Pulmonary Embolism or Pneumonia (most likely diagnosis based on Shortness of Breath)
exam: Dyspneic, speaking in short phrases if severe
026.clinical_tip: ABG or pulse ox + ECG + CXR in the first minutes.
026.objective: Stabilize hypoxia. Differentiate pulmonary vs cardiac vs metabolic.

interventions.label: Order a D-dimer test and/or pulmonary angiography for PE suspicion
Order sputum culture, chest CT scan for pneumonia suspicion
Oxygen
interventions.why: The patient presents with shortness of breath, which is a symptom requiring immediate oxygen supplementation.

interventions.label: IV access ×2 large-bore
interventions.why: Large bore IV access is necessary for rapid administration of fluids and medications in the event of an emergency.

027.title: Fatigue
027.diagnosis: A comprehensive workup is necessary due to the unknown cause of fatigue.
exam: Fatigue — exam tailored to chief complaint on arrival.
Soft, non-distended, no focal peritoneal signs on initial exam.
Alert and oriented unless perfusion or metabolic derangement present.
No acute rash; capillary refill and perfusion assessed.
027.clinical_tip: Stabilize first — ABCs, IV access, monitor, then targeted workup.
027.objective: Identify and treat life threats within the time limit.

interventions.label: Obtain complete blood count (CBC) and metabolic panel
interventions.why: These tests are useful in identifying anemia, electrolyte imbalances, or kidney dysfunction that could cause fatigue.

interventions.label: Consider additional testing based on initial findings such as thyroid function tests, vitamin B12/Folate levels, and comprehensive metabolic panel with liver enzymes
Oxygen
interventions.why: Patient presents with fatigue, which could be a symptom of multiple conditions including hypoglycemia. Oxygen is crucial to support patient's respiration.

interventions.label: IV access ×2
interventions.why: To administer necessary fluids and medications promptly.

interventions.label: Glucose check
interventions.why: Fatigue can also be a symptom of low blood sugar.

028.title: Pelvic Pain
028.diagnosis: Possible Gynecologic or Genitourinary Disorder, Abdominal Pain of Unknown Origin (APOO), Ovarian Torsion (in women of reproductive age), Pelvic Inflammatory Disease (PID), Ectopic Pregnancy, Appendicitis (if right lower quadrant pain is present), or other abdominal pathologies
028.vitalsText: Initial Vital Signs Day 1 @ 9:00 Temperature: 37.0 C (98.6 degrees F) Pulse: 110 beat/min, Tachycardic Respiratory rate: 22 /minute Blood pressure, systolic: 140 mm Hg Blood pressure, diastolic: 92 mm Hg Height: 175.3 cm (69.0 in) Weight: 65.8 kg (145 lb) Body Mass Index: 21.4 kg/m2
exam: complaining of pelvic pain for the last month.
Abdominal tenderness pattern matches history; no rigid abdomen documented yet.
Alert and oriented unless perfusion or metabolic derangement present.
No acute rash; capillary refill and perfusion assessed.
028.clinical_tip: Positive pregnancy test changes everything — ectopic until proven otherwise.
028.objective: Rule out ectopic, torsion, and rupture.

interventions.label: Order urine pregnancy test in women of childbearing age
Order ultrasound for pelvis and/or abdomen
Consider lab tests for sexually transmitted diseases (STDs) if PID is suspected
Consult Obstetrics & Gynecology or General Surgery as needed
Oxygen
interventions.why: The patient is experiencing pelvic pain, which can be a symptom of various conditions. However, initial priority should always be given to maintaining oxygenation and stabilizing the patient's condition.

interventions.label: IV access ×2 large-bore
interventions.why: Large-bore IV access is essential for administering fluids and medications quickly in case of emergency.

easy.intro: Initial History Reason(s) for visit: Pelvic pain History of present Illness: This is a 20-year-old previously healthy female presenting to your office complaining of pelvic pain for the last month. She rates the pain as a constant 7/10 sharp pain which worsens when she moves. She was encouraged by her boyfriend to come to you because she is having extreme pain during sex. Ibuprofen "takes the edge off." The pain is severe enough that she reports having difficulty sleeping. Her pain worsens during her menstruation and that the pain is "unbearable." She admits that she has had unprotected sex with her boyfriend a few times, but reports that she is taking an oral contraceptive. She denies multiple sexual partners in the past. She has had a few episodes of vomiting and still feels nauseous. No fevers or chills. She also reports pain when having a bowel movement. She denies any vaginal discharge. Past Medical History: Current medications: Oral Contraceptive Allergies: None Vaccinations: Received Childhood vaccinations. Women's health: She has never been to a gynecologist. Family History: The patient's mother has a history of breast cancer in her 50s. Her father had a 'heart attack' in his 40s. Marital/Family: The patient is the oldest of 3 children. Both of her siblings are healthy without medical problems. Personal habits: She admits to smoking 2 cigarettes a day to help lose weight. She also admits to smoking marijuana occasionally at parties. She also drinks alcohol once a week with friends. Occupation/educational: She is a college student and currently studying Art History and would like to be a teacher. Recreational: She enjoys to paint and train dogs. 029.title: Vaginal Bleeding
exam: Vaginal Bleeding — exam tailored to chief complaint on arrival.
Soft, non-distended, no focal peritoneal signs on initial exam.
Alert and oriented unless perfusion or metabolic derangement present.
No acute rash; capillary refill and perfusion assessed.
029.clinical_tip: Positive pregnancy test changes everything — ectopic until proven otherwise.
029.objective: Rule out ectopic, torsion, and rupture.

interventions.label: Lithium
interventions.why: Lithium is commonly used in treating chronic kidney disease, especially in managing nephrogenic diabetes insipidus.

interventions.label: Frusemide
interventions.why: Frusemide may help manage volume overload in patients with CKD by reducing sodium and water retention.

interventions.label: Oxygen
interventions.why: The patient is experiencing vaginal bleeding, and immediate oxygen support is necessary to ensure adequate oxygenation.

interventions.label: IV access ×2 large-bore
interventions.why: To administer fluids and medications as needed in case of severe bleeding or other complications.

easy.clinicalTip: Teaching hint: Positive pregnancy test changes everything — ectopic until proven otherwise.
easy.objective: Rule out ectopic, torsion, and rupture. Start with stabilization, then targeted testing.

030.title: Pain in Right Arm
030.diagnosis: Acute Coronary Syndrome (ACS) or Pericarditis are common differentials for right arm pain, however further evaluation is necessary
exam: Pain in Right Arm — exam tailored to chief complaint on arrival.
Soft, non-distended, no focal peritoneal signs on initial exam.
Alert and oriented unless perfusion or metabolic derangement present.
No acute rash; capillary refill and perfusion assessed.
030.clinical_tip: Stabilize first — ABCs, IV access, monitor, then targeted workup.
030.objective: Identify and treat life threats within the time limit.

interventions.label: Obtain ECG to assess for acute coronary syndrome and other cardiac abnormalities.
Order cardiac enzymes (Troponin, CK-MB) to evaluate for myocardial injury in ACS.
Oxygen
interventions.why: The patient is experiencing pain in the right arm which could potentially be indicative of a cardiac event, and oxygen administration is an essential first step in stabilizing the patient.

interventions.label: IV access ×2
interventions.why: Administering intravenous fluids quickly is crucial for addressing any potential electrolyte imbalances or other fluid-related issues that may arise.

interventions.label: Nasal cannula
interventions.why: This order is provided as a safe and non-invasive method to deliver oxygen, which should be done immediately upon the patient's arrival.

031.title: Dizziness and Fatigue
031.diagnosis: Might be related to Cardiovascular, Neurological, or Endocrine disorders, but without definitive diagnosis
exam: Dizziness and Fatigue — exam tailored to chief complaint on arrival.
Soft, non-distended, no focal peritoneal signs on initial exam.
Alert and oriented unless perfusion or metabolic derangement present.
No acute rash; capillary refill and perfusion assessed.
031.clinical_tip: Fingerstick glucose before CT — hypoglycemia mimics stroke.

interventions.label: Obtain a complete blood count (CBC), basic metabolic panel (BMP), coagulation profile, and comprehensive metabolic panel.
interventions.why: To evaluate possible causes of dizziness and fatigue, including anemia, electrolyte imbalances, and endocrine disorders.

interventions.label: Brain imaging such as MRI or CT scan if neurological symptoms are severe or persistent.
24-hour Holter monitor for further evaluation of potential arrhythmias.
Oxygen
interventions.why: The patient is experiencing dizziness and fatigue, which could be signs of hypoxia. Oxygen should be administered immediately.

interventions.label: IV access ×2
interventions.why: Intravenous access is essential for administering fluids, medications, and drawing blood samples.

interventions.label: Follow-up Actions (Next 30 Seconds)
interventions.why: Universal lab tests such as CBC, CMP, UA, and EKG will provide valuable information about the patient's condition.

interventions.label: CMP/BMP
interventions.why: This order includes electrolytes, renal, and liver status which can help diagnose various conditions causing dizziness and fatigue.

easy.clinicalTip: Teaching hint: Fingerstick glucose before CT — hypoglycemia mimics stroke.
easy.objective: Stabilize ABCs. Identify reversible causes. Start with stabilization, then targeted testing.

032.title: Abdominal Pain
032.diagnosis: Acute Abdomen (e.g., appendicitis, cholecystitis, mesenteric ischemia)
032.vitalsText: Initial Vital Signs Day 1 @ 18:00 Temperature: 37.2 C (98.9 degrees F) Pulse: 106 beat/min, Tachycardic Respiratory rate: 20 /minute Blood pressure, systolic: 114 mm Hg Blood pressure, diastolic: 79 mm Hg Height: 160.0 cm (63.0 in) Weight: 80.5 kg (177.4 lb) Body Mass Index: 31.4 kg/m2
exam: presents to the emergency room with abdominal pain, lethargy, and vomiting x2 days.
Abdominal tenderness pattern matches history; no rigid abdomen documented yet.
Alert and oriented unless perfusion or metabolic derangement present.
No acute rash; capillary refill and perfusion assessed.
032.clinical_tip: Elderly and immunocompromised may have minimal exam findings until rupture.
032.objective: Resuscitate, image, involve surgery early if indicated.

interventions.label: Obtain vital signs and perform a physical exam
interventions.why: To quickly assess the patient's overall condition

interventions.label: Order abdominal x-ray (AP and lateral views)
interventions.why: To help identify free air under the diaphragm, possible calcifications or masses

interventions.label: Order complete blood count (CBC)
interventions.why: To evaluate for anemia, leukocytosis, or thrombocytopenia

interventions.label: Order electrolyte panel, liver function tests (LFTs), amylase, and lipase
interventions.why: To help identify pancreatitis or other potential causes of abdominal pain

interventions.label: Consider ordering urinalysis
interventions.why: To evaluate for potential kidney involvement or infection

interventions.label: Consult General Surgery for possible surgical intervention
interventions.why: If the physical exam and initial diagnostic results suggest a surgical emergency

interventions.label: order1
interventions.why: Reason for correct order, if any

interventions.label: CBC · Lipase · LFTs · UA
interventions.why: Narrow differential: biliary, pancreatic, UTI, medical.

interventions.label: CT Abdomen/Pelvis
interventions.why: High yield for appendicitis, diverticulitis, obstruction.

interventions.label: Antibiotics if indicated
interventions.why: Give before OR if sepsis or perforation suspected.

easy.intro: Initial History Reason(s) for visit: Abdominal Pain History of present Illness: A 22-year-old female presents to the emergency room with abdominal pain, lethargy, and vomiting x2 days. Her vomiting is non-bloody. Her abdominal pain is diffuse, which she rates as a 6/10. She also claims to have had an increased appetite prior to having these symptoms. She also claims that she’s been developing blurry vision, dizziness, and drowsiness. 033.title: Altered Mental Status
033.diagnosis: Altered Mental Status - Possible CNS infection, metabolic disorder, or toxic-metabolic encephalopathy
033.vitalsText: Initial Vital Signs Day 1 @ 11:00 Temperature: 36.8 C (98.2 degrees F) Pulse: 88 beats/min. Regular rhythm Respiratory rate: 18 /minute Blood pressure, systolic: 115 mm Hg Blood pressure, diastolic: 85 mm Hg Height: 180cm (70.9 in) Weight: 91.0 kg (200.6 lb) Body Mass Index: 27.9 kg/m2
exam: Decreased engagement and cognitive change compared with reported baseline.
Soft, non-distended, no focal peritoneal signs on initial exam.
Altered mental status with attention and command-following documented.
No acute rash; capillary refill and perfusion assessed.
033.clinical_tip: Fingerstick glucose before CT — hypoglycemia mimics stroke.

interventions.label: Obtain lumbar puncture for cerebrospinal fluid analysis (if patient is stable and no contraindications)
Maintain airway, administer oxygen as needed, and closely monitor vital signs
Oxygen
interventions.why: Altered Mental Status could be due to hypoxia, so providing oxygen is essential.

interventions.label: Order: Order: time-sensitive interventions first; (oxygen, IV access, glucose check)
interventions.why: Immediate stabilization and assessment of vital signs is crucial in managing patients with altered mental status.

easy.intro: Initial History Reason(s) for visit: Altered mental status History of present Illness: A 70-year-old Caucasian man comes to the Emergency room because his family members are concerned that something is wrong. The patient has been having behavioral changes over a period of three or four weeks. He used to be very active and managed all of his financial affairs. He now barely talks, stares at the wall for hours at a time, and sleeps most of the day. Four weeks ago, the patient got up to urinate and fell before reaching the bathroom. He was found on the floor the next morning by family members. He was taken to his family doctor and was discharged with a prescription to buy a cane. Since that time, he has been having problems walking. There have been no fevers or night sweats. The patient is currently in no pain. Past Medical History: Chronic alcohol abuse; cataracts. Hospitalization Procedures: Hemorrhoidectomy 1 year ago. Last colonoscopy was 4 years ago which demonstrated no abnormalities. Current medications: Aspirin 81mg PO daily Allergies: None Vaccinations: Up to date Family History: Father: Deceased due to a 'heart attack'. Mother: Diagnosed with type 2 diabetes mellitus and died of natural causes. The patient has no siblings. Social History: Marital/Family: Widowed; he has three children. Personal habits: the patient smokes 1 Pack/day. He currently does not drink alcohol but was a chronic alcoholic for 30 years. Denies use of illegal drugs or misuse of medications. Occupation/educational: He is a retired Army human resources manager. He is a high school graduate. Recreational: The patient likes to spend time with his children and enjoys collecting stamps. 034.title: Headache
034.diagnosis: Unknown Headache: Migraine, Tension-type headache, Cluster headache, or other secondary causes
034.vitalsText: Initial Vital Signs Day 1 @ 9:00 Temperature: 38.3 C (101.0 degrees F) Pulse: 88 beats/min. Regular rhythm Respiratory rate: 18 /minute Blood pressure, systolic: 118 mm Hg Blood pressure, diastolic: 70 mm Hg Height: 175.3 cm (69.0 in) Weight: 65.8 kg (145 lb) Body Mass Index: 21.4 kg/m2
exam: presents to your office with a an occipital headache, mild vertigo, and a tendency to fall when walking for 6 days.
Abdominal tenderness pattern matches history; no rigid abdomen documented yet.
Mental status intact; cranial nerves and neck stiffness assessed.
Skin rash morphology and distribution documented.
034.clinical_tip: Thunderclap headache = SAH workup. Do not send home without considering red flags.
034.objective: Rule out SAH, meningitis, mass, and hypertensive emergency.

interventions.label: Obtain a detailed history including symptoms, triggers, and family history
Perform a physical examination focusing on neurological assessment
Order laboratory tests (CBC, ESR, glucose, electrolytes, liver function tests) to rule out secondary causes if clinical suspicion arises
Consider imaging (CT or MRI) if red flags are present (e.g., sudden onset, severe headache, focal neurological deficits)
Refer patient to a neurologist for further evaluation and management if necessary
CMP/BMP
interventions.why: This order helps to evaluate the patient's renal and liver function, electrolyte levels which can be useful in diagnosing various conditions causing headaches such as metabolic imbalances, renal diseases, or hepatic diseases.

easy.intro: Initial History Reason(s) for visit: Headache History of present Illness: A 55-year-old female with a past medical history of hypertension presents to your office with a an occipital headache, mild vertigo, and a tendency to fall when walking for 6 days. She states that she never had any problems walking or being off-balance. Eight days ago, she stated that she had a pink rash on her arms and torso and felt feverish. The rash improved after a couple days, but the headache and tendency to fall persisted. She had nausea, but she denied any vomiting. She feels weak and "under the weather." She denies travel, but went hiking 2 weeks ago nearby her house which she routinely does every summer. She denies any history of migraines. She will occasionally get a tension headache which she will treat with Ibuprofen. She has never had a headache that has lasted this long. She denies any trauma to her head. She denies any sick contacts. She denies any recent antibiotic use. She is very concerned about her symptoms. PMH: Past Medical History: Hypertension. The patient recently had a colonoscopy screening which was normal. Current medications: HCTZ 25mg PO daily Allergies: None Vaccinations: Received Childhood vaccinations. Women's health: The patient cannot remember when her last pap smear was done. Family History: Her father has a history of Hypertension and died of a stroke. Her mother has no health problems and is still living. Social History: Marital/Family: She is married with one child. She lives in Gaithersburg, Maryland. Personal habits: The patient denies smoking. She lives an active lifestyle. She drinks alcohol socially. She denies use of illegal drugs or misuse of medications. Occupation/educational: She works as a public relations specialist. She is a college graduate with a BS in communication. Recreational: The patient enjoys to bike and hike. She loves the outdoors and spends as much time as she is able. standard.intro: Initial History Reason(s) for visit: Headache History of present Illness: A 55-year-old female with a past medical history of hypertension presents to your office with a an occipital headache, mild vertigo, and a tendency to fall when walking for 6 days. She states that she never had any problems walking or being off-balance. Eight days ago, she stated that she had a pink rash on her arms and torso and felt feverish. The rash improved after a couple days, but the headache and tendency to fall persisted. She had nausea, but she denied any vomiting. She feels weak and "under the weather." She denies travel, but went hiking 2 weeks ago nearby her house which she routinely does every summer. She denies any history of migraines. She will occasionally get a tension headache which she will treat with Ibuprofen. She has never had a headache that has lasted this long. She denies any trauma to her head. She denies any sick contacts. She denies any recent antibiotic use. She is very concerned about her symptoms. PMH: Past Medical History: Hypertension. The patient recently had a colonoscopy screening which was normal. Current medications: HCTZ 25mg PO daily Allergies: None Vaccinations: Received Childhood vaccinations. Women's health: The patient cannot remember when her last pap smear was done. Family History: Her father has a history of Hypertension and died of a stroke. Her mother has no health problems and is still living. Social History: Marital/Family: She is married with one child. She lives in Gaithersburg, Maryland. Personal habits: The patient denies smoking. She lives an active lifestyle. She drinks alcohol socially. She denies use of illegal drugs or misuse of medications. Occupation/educational: She works as a public relations specialist. She is a college graduate with a BS in communication. Recreational: The patient enjoys to bike and hike. She loves the outdoors and spends as much time as she is able. 035.title: Seizure
035.diagnosis: Status Epilepticus or Structural Brain Abnormality (pending further workup)
exam: Seizure — exam tailored to chief complaint on arrival.
Soft, non-distended, no focal peritoneal signs on initial exam.
Neurologic exam focused on mental status and focal deficits.
No acute rash; capillary refill and perfusion assessed.
035.clinical_tip: Fingerstick glucose before CT — hypoglycemia mimics stroke.

interventions.label: Obtain stat head CT to rule out structural brain abnormalities or acute process
Administration of benzodiazepines (e.g., lorazepam) for seizure control
Consult Neurology for further management, workup, and long-term treatment options
Monitor vital signs, oxygenation, and neurological status
Oxygen
interventions.why: To provide supplemental oxygen if the patient is having difficulty breathing

interventions.label: IV access
interventions.why: For administration of fluids, medications, and blood products as needed

interventions.label: monitor
interventions.why: To continuously monitor the patient's vital signs

interventions.label: pulse ox
interventions.why: To assess the patient's oxygen saturation level

interventions.label: CBC
interventions.why: To evaluate for anemia, infection, or other hematologic abnormalities

interventions.label: CMP
interventions.why: To evaluate renal and liver function, as well as electrolyte levels

interventions.label: glucose
interventions.why: To check for hypoglycemia or hyperglycemia which can trigger seizures

interventions.label: tox screen
interventions.why: To rule out toxicology as a cause of the seizure

easy.clinicalTip: Teaching hint: Fingerstick glucose before CT — hypoglycemia mimics stroke.
easy.objective: Stabilize ABCs. Identify reversible causes. Start with stabilization, then targeted testing.

standard.clinicalTip: Fingerstick glucose before CT — hypoglycemia mimics stroke.
standard.objective: Stabilize ABCs. Identify reversible causes.

036.title: Headache
036.diagnosis: Primary Headache Disorders (such as Migraine, Tension-type headache, Cluster headache) or Secondary Headaches (due to intracranial or extracranial conditions) should be considered
036.vitalsText: Initial Vital Signs Day 1 @ 9:00 Temperature: 38.3 C (101.0 degrees F) Pulse: 88 beats/min. Regular rhythm Respiratory rate: 18 /minute Blood pressure, systolic: 118 mm Hg Blood pressure, diastolic: 70 mm Hg Height: 175.3 cm (69.0 in) Weight: 65.8 kg (145 lb) Body Mass Index: 21.4 kg/m2
exam: presents to your office with a an occipital headache, mild vertigo, and a tendency to fall when walking for 6 days.
Abdominal tenderness pattern matches history; no rigid abdomen documented yet.
Mental status intact; cranial nerves and neck stiffness assessed.
Skin rash morphology and distribution documented.
036.clinical_tip: Thunderclap headache = SAH workup. Do not send home without considering red flags.
036.objective: Rule out SAH, meningitis, mass, and hypertensive emergency.

interventions.label: Obtain a detailed history and perform a physical examination
Order routine laboratory tests: CBC, Electrolytes, Glucose, Liver function tests, Coagulation profile (if there is any indication of bleeding or bruising)
Imaging studies (CT or MRI) if the headache is severe, persistent, or associated with neurological symptoms
Refer to a Neurologist for further evaluation and management if necessary
Oxygen
interventions.why: To ensure adequate oxygenation in case of hypoxia or compromised respiration.

interventions.label: Order
interventions.why: To initiate the autopilot protocol with essential orders for initial assessment and stabilization.

interventions.label: Never forget
interventions.why: Pregnancy test should always be considered in women of childbearing age.

037.title: Fatigue
037.diagnosis: Unknown fatigue could be caused by a wide range of conditions including metabolic disorders, infectious diseases, autoimmune disorders, neurological disorders, and psychological issues. Initial workup should focus on common causes.
exam: Fatigue — exam tailored to chief complaint on arrival.
Soft, non-distended, no focal peritoneal signs on initial exam.
Alert and oriented unless perfusion or metabolic derangement present.
No acute rash; capillary refill and perfusion assessed.
037.clinical_tip: Stabilize first — ABCs, IV access, monitor, then targeted workup.
037.objective: Identify and treat life threats within the time limit.

interventions.label: Laboratory tests: CBC, comprehensive metabolic panel, ESR/CRP, HIV, Hepatitis panels, Lyme disease test, thyroid function tests
Imaging studies (if clinically indicated): Chest X-ray, MRI/CT if neurological symptoms present
Oxygen
interventions.why: To provide oxygen to the patient, essential for maintaining life

interventions.label: IV access ×2
interventions.why: To ensure adequate intravenous access for administration of fluids and medications

interventions.label: glucose check
interventions.why: To assess blood glucose level, which can be a cause of fatigue

039.title: Epigastric pain

exam: Epigastric pain — exam tailored to chief complaint on arrival.

Alert and oriented unless perfusion or metabolic derangement present.
Warm skin with fever; no purpura on initial survey.

040.title: Chest Pain
040.diagnosis: Chest pain of unknown etiology, potential causes include acute coronary syndrome, pulmonary embolism, aortic dissection, pneumonia, or esophageal disorders.
040.vitalsText: Initial Vital Signs Day 1 @ 16:00 Temperature: 37.0 C (98.6 degrees F) Pulse: 120 beats/min, Weak Respiratory rate: 34 /minute Blood pressure, systolic: 100 mm Hg Blood pressure, diastolic: 60 mm Hg Height: 183cm (72.0 in) Weight: 97.5 kg (215 lb) Body Mass Index: 29.1 kg/m2
exam: Chest Pain — exam tailored to chief complaint on arrival.
Soft, non-distended, no focal peritoneal signs on initial exam.
Alert and oriented unless perfusion or metabolic derangement present.
No acute rash; capillary refill and perfusion assessed.
040.clinical_tip: Until proven otherwise, treat unstable chest pain as ACS.
040.objective: Rule out life threats. ECG within 10 minutes.

interventions.label: Obtain laboratory tests: Complete blood count (CBC), cardiac enzymes (Troponin I/T, CK-MB), D-dimer
Order chest X-ray and possibly computed tomography angiography (CTA) or echocardiography depending on initial results and clinical suspicion
Consult Cardiology for further management and possible stress testing if indicated
Continuously monitor vital signs, oxygen saturation, and ECG monitoring
Administer sublingual nitrate if patient has persistent chest pain and no contraindications
Oxygen
interventions.why: The patient is experiencing chest pain, which could be a sign of a life-threatening condition. Therefore, it's crucial to provide oxygen immediately.

interventions.label: IV access ×2
interventions.why: To administer necessary fluids and medications quickly in case of an emergency.

easy.intro: Initial History Reason(s) for visit: Chest pain; respiratory distress History of present Illness: The patient, a 65-year-old accountant, is brought to the emergency department by ambulance from the trucking company, where he works. Ten minutes prior to the ambulance’s arrival, the patient developed excruciating, sharp pain on the right side of his chest accompanied by marked respiratory distress. He rates the pain as an 8 on a 10-point scale. The pain increases on respiration, and he is unable to answer questions. A coworker who accompanied the patient to the hospital says that this has never happened to the patient in the past. Coworker mentions patient has a past medical history of emphysema and asthma. Oxygen was administered during transport.

easy.intro: Initial History Reason(s) for visit: Chest pain; respiratory distress History of present Illness: The patient, a 65-year-old accountant, is brought to the emergency department by ambulance from the trucking company, where he works. Ten minutes prior to the ambulance’s arrival, the patient developed excruciating, sharp pain on the right side of his chest accompanied by marked respiratory distress. He rates the pain as an 8 on a 10-point scale. The pain increases on respiration, and he is unable to answer questions. A coworker who accompanied the patient to the hospital says that this has never happened to the patient in the past. Coworker mentions patient has a past medical history of emphysema and asthma. Oxygen was administered during transport.

interventions.label: Obtain chest X-ray
interventions.why: Helps in identifying possible pneumonia, mass, or consolidation

interventions.label: Order complete blood count (CBC) and basic metabolic panel (BMP)
interventions.why: Assesses for signs of infection, anemia, or other systemic issues

interventions.label: Order sputum culture if expectorated sputum is present
interventions.why: Helps identify specific bacterial pathogens responsible for the respiratory symptoms

interventions.label: Start broad-spectrum antibiotics empirically, pending culture results
interventions.why: Provides initial treatment while waiting for culture results

interventions.label: Consult Pulmonology for further evaluation and management
interventions.why: Ensures comprehensive management of potential underlying pulmonary conditions

interventions.label: If fever or signs of pneumonia are present, consider ordering a chest CT scan
interventions.why: Helps in identifying complications such as abscesses, pleural effusions, or lung cancer

interventions.label: Oxygen
interventions.why: Cough may indicate respiratory distress, thus oxygen is crucial.

interventions.label: IV access ×2 large-bore
interventions.why: Vital for administration of fluids, medications, and blood products if needed.
