049.title: Headache
049.diagnosis: Primary Headache (Tension-type or Migraine) until further diagnostic evaluation
049.vitalsText: Initial Vital Signs Day 1 @ 9:00 Temperature: 38.3 C (101.0 degrees F) Pulse: 88 beats/min. Regular rhythm Respiratory rate: 18 /minute Blood pressure, systolic: 118 mm Hg Blood pressure, diastolic: 70 mm Hg Height: 175.3 cm (69.0 in) Weight: 65.8 kg (145 lb) Body Mass Index: 21.4 kg/m2
exam: presents to your office with a an occipital headache, mild vertigo, and a tendency to fall when walking for 6 days.
Abdominal tenderness pattern matches history; no rigid abdomen documented yet.
Mental status intact; cranial nerves and neck stiffness assessed.
Skin rash morphology and distribution documented.
049.clinical_tip: Thunderclap headache = SAH workup. Do not send home without considering red flags.
049.objective: Rule out SAH, meningitis, mass, and hypertensive emergency.

interventions.label: Obtain a detailed history, including family history of headaches, trigger factors, and associated symptoms
Perform a physical examination focusing on the neurological system
Order appropriate laboratory tests (CBC, ESR, glucose, electrolytes, thyroid function tests) to rule out secondary causes if necessary
Consider imaging studies such as CT or MRI of the brain if symptoms persist, worsen, or are atypical
If no secondary cause is identified and headaches meet criteria for migraine or tension-type headache, initiate appropriate treatment (e.g., analgesics, antiemetics, antimigraine drugs)
Refer the patient to a neurologist for further evaluation and management if necessary
Oxygen
interventions.why: Immediate oxygen is essential for any patient presenting with headache to ensure adequate oxygenation.

interventions.label: Order: Order: time-sensitive interventions first; (oxygen, IV access, glucose check)
interventions.why: Time-sensitive interventions such as oxygen and IV access are crucial in the initial management of a patient presenting with headache.

easy.intro: Initial History Reason(s) for visit: Headache History of present Illness: A 55-year-old female with a past medical history of hypertension presents to your office with a an occipital headache, mild vertigo, and a tendency to fall when walking for 6 days. She states that she never had any problems walking or being off-balance. Eight days ago, she stated that she had a pink rash on her arms and torso and felt feverish. The rash improved after a couple days, but the headache and tendency to fall persisted. She had nausea, but she denied any vomiting. She feels weak and "under the weather." She denies travel, but went hiking 2 weeks ago nearby her house which she routinely does every summer. She denies any history of migraines. She will occasionally get a tension headache which she will treat with Ibuprofen. She has never had a headache that has lasted this long. She denies any trauma to her head. She denies any sick contacts. She denies any recent antibiotic use. She is very concerned about her symptoms. PMH: Past Medical History: Hypertension. The patient recently had a colonoscopy screening which was normal. Current medications: HCTZ 25mg PO daily Allergies: None Vaccinations: Received Childhood vaccinations. Women's health: The patient cannot remember when her last pap smear was done. Family History: Her father has a history of Hypertension and died of a stroke. Her mother has no health problems and is still living. Social History: Marital/Family: She is married with one child. She lives in Gaithersburg, Maryland. Personal habits: The patient denies smoking. She lives an active lifestyle. She drinks alcohol socially. She denies use of illegal drugs or misuse of medications. Occupation/educational: She works as a public relations specialist. She is a college graduate with a BS in communication. Recreational: The patient enjoys to bike and hike. She loves the outdoors and spends as much time as she is able. Review of systems: General: See HPI Skin: See HPI HEENT: Negative Cardiorespiratory: Negative Gastrointestinal: See HPI Genitourinary: Negative Neuropsychiatric: See HPI --- Chart tabs --- * Chart: Progress Notes → presentation_5_Headache_chart_Progress_Notes.png * Chart: Vital Signs → presentation_5_Headache_chart_Vital_Signs.png

050.title: Headache
050.diagnosis: Primary Headache (e.g., Migraine, Tension-type headache), Secondary Headache (e.g., Intracranial hemorrhage, meningitis) should be considered
050.vitalsText: Initial Vital Signs Day 1 @ 9:00 Temperature: 38.3 C (101.0 degrees F) Pulse: 88 beats/min. Regular rhythm Respiratory rate: 18 /minute Blood pressure, systolic: 118 mm Hg Blood pressure, diastolic: 70 mm Hg Height: 175.3 cm (69.0 in) Weight: 65.8 kg (145 lb) Body Mass Index: 21.4 kg/m2
exam: presents to your office with a an occipital headache, mild vertigo, and a tendency to fall when walking for 6 days.
Abdominal tenderness pattern matches history; no rigid abdomen documented yet.
Mental status intact; cranial nerves and neck stiffness assessed.
Skin rash morphology and distribution documented.
050.clinical_tip: Thunderclap headache = SAH workup. Do not send home without considering red flags.
050.objective: Rule out SAH, meningitis, mass, and hypertensive emergency.

interventions.label: Obtain a detailed history including onset, duration, location, severity, and any associated symptoms
interventions.why: To gather necessary information to guide further diagnostic workup

interventions.label: Physical examination focusing on neurological assessment
interventions.why: To assess for signs of intracranial pathology or secondary headaches

interventions.label: Order laboratory tests: Complete Blood Count (CBC), Electrolytes, Glucose, Liver Function Tests (LFTs), and Coagulation studies
interventions.why: To rule out systemic causes of headache such as infections, metabolic disorders, or coagulopathies

interventions.label: Imaging studies: Non-contrast CT scan of the brain if there are signs or symptoms suggesting intracranial pathology. If CT is normal or inconclusive, consider MRI with and without contrast
interventions.why: To assess for intracranial abnormalities or structural lesions

interventions.label: Consider referral to a neurologist for further evaluation and management
interventions.why: For specialized evaluation and management of the patient's headache

interventions.label: Treat patient symptomatically with analgesics (acetaminophen, ibuprofen) and anti-emetics if necessary
interventions.why: To provide relief from symptoms while awaiting diagnostic results

interventions.label: order1
interventions.why: Reason for the correct order is not visible in the image provided.

interventions.label: Oxygen
interventions.why: The patient is presenting with a headache, which could be associated with severe distress or altered mental status. Therefore, oxygen should be administered immediately.

interventions.label: IV access ×2
interventions.why: It is essential to secure large-bore IV access for fluid resuscitation, administration of medications, and monitoring the patient's condition.

interventions.label: universal labs + imaging
interventions.why: In this case, a broad approach is necessary to evaluate potential causes for the headache. Universal labs (CBC, CMP, UA) and an initial imaging study such as a CT head non-contrast STAT would be appropriate.

051.title: Altered Mental Status
051.diagnosis: Possible diagnoses may include acute intoxication, encephalopathy, infection, metabolic disorders, or central nervous system (CNS) trauma.
051.vitalsText: Initial Vital Signs Day 1 @ 11:00 Temperature: 36.8 C (98.2 degrees F) Pulse: 88 beats/min. Regular rhythm Respiratory rate: 18 /minute Blood pressure, systolic: 115 mm Hg Blood pressure, diastolic: 85 mm Hg Height: 180cm (70.9 in) Weight: 91.0 kg (200.6 lb) Body Mass Index: 27.9 kg/m2
exam: Decreased engagement and cognitive change compared with reported baseline.
Soft, non-distended, no focal peritoneal signs on initial exam.
Altered mental status with attention and command-following documented.
No acute rash; capillary refill and perfusion assessed.
051.clinical_tip: Fingerstick glucose before CT — hypoglycemia mimics stroke.

interventions.label: Laboratory tests: Complete blood count (CBC), comprehensive metabolic panel (CMP), toxicology screen, and coagulation studies
Imaging: Non-contrast head CT to rule out acute intracranial pathologies
Oxygen
interventions.why: The patient is exhibiting altered mental status, which may indicate hypoxia. Therefore, oxygen should be administered immediately.

interventions.label: Order: Order: time-sensitive interventions first; (oxygen, IV access, glucose check)
interventions.why: In any critical situation, it's essential to address life-threatening issues promptly. Oxygen, IV access, and a quick glucose check are crucial initial steps.

easy.intro: Initial History Reason(s) for visit: Altered mental status History of present Illness: A 70-year-old Caucasian man comes to the Emergency room because his family members are concerned that something is wrong. The patient has been having behavioral changes over a period of three or four weeks. He used to be very active and managed all of his financial affairs. He now barely talks, stares at the wall for hours at a time, and sleeps most of the day. Four weeks ago, the patient got up to urinate and fell before reaching the bathroom. He was found on the floor the next morning by family members. He was taken to his family doctor and was discharged with a prescription to buy a cane. Since that time, he has been having problems walking. There have been no fevers or night sweats. The patient is currently in no pain. Past Medical History: Chronic alcohol abuse; cataracts. Hospitalization Procedures: Hemorrhoidectomy 1 year ago. Last colonoscopy was 4 years ago which demonstrated no abnormalities. Current medications: Aspirin 81mg PO daily Allergies: None Vaccinations: Up to date Family History: Father: Deceased due to a 'heart attack'. Mother: Diagnosed with type 2 diabetes mellitus and died of natural causes. The patient has no siblings. Social History: Marital/Family: Widowed; he has three children. Personal habits: the patient smokes 1 Pack/day. He currently does not drink alcohol but was a chronic alcoholic for 30 years. Denies use of illegal drugs or misuse of medications. Occupation/educational: He is a retired Army human resources manager. He is a high school graduate. Recreational: The patient likes to spend time with his children and enjoys collecting stamps. 052.title: Chest Pain
052.diagnosis: Acute Coronary Syndrome (ACS) or other cardiac conditions are the most likely diagnoses for a patient presenting with Chest Pain of unknown cause.
052.vitalsText: Initial Vital Signs Day 1 @ 16:00 Temperature: 37.0 C (98.6 degrees F) Pulse: 120 beats/min, Weak Respiratory rate: 34 /minute Blood pressure, systolic: 100 mm Hg Blood pressure, diastolic: 60 mm Hg Height: 183cm (72.0 in) Weight: 97.5 kg (215 lb) Body Mass Index: 29.1 kg/m2
exam: Chest Pain — exam tailored to chief complaint on arrival.
Soft, non-distended, no focal peritoneal signs on initial exam.
Alert and oriented unless perfusion or metabolic derangement present.
No acute rash; capillary refill and perfusion assessed.
052.clinical_tip: Until proven otherwise, treat unstable chest pain as ACS.
052.objective: Rule out life threats. ECG within 10 minutes.

interventions.label: Order blood tests including cardiac enzymes and troponin
Administer oxygen if patient is short of breath or oxygen saturation is low
Consider obtaining a chest X-ray for further imaging
Oxygen
interventions.why: Chest pain can be a symptom of various life-threatening conditions. Oxygen is essential to stabilize the patient immediately.

interventions.label: Order: Order: time-sensitive interventions first; (oxygen, IV access, glucose check)
interventions.why: As stated in 'Life-Saving Interventions That Must Be Ordered Immediately', critical principle for Step 3 CCS is that if you delay stabilization while ordering labs, your score plummets even with a correct diagnosis.

easy.intro: Initial History Reason(s) for visit: Chest pain; respiratory distress History of present Illness: The patient, a 65-year-old accountant, is brought to the emergency department by ambulance from the trucking company, where he works. Ten minutes prior to the ambulance’s arrival, the patient developed excruciating, sharp pain on the right side of his chest accompanied by marked respiratory distress. He rates the pain as an 8 on a 10-point scale. The pain increases on respiration, and he is unable to answer questions. A coworker who accompanied the patient to the hospital says that this has never happened to the patient in the past. Coworker mentions patient has a past medical history of emphysema and asthma. Oxygen was administered during transport.

053.title: Shortness of Breath

exam: Dyspneic, speaking in short phrases if severe
053.clinical_tip: ABG or pulse ox + ECG + CXR in the first minutes.
053.objective: Stabilize hypoxia. Differentiate pulmonary vs cardiac vs metabolic.

054.title: Yellow Baby
exam: Yellow Baby — exam tailored to chief complaint on arrival.
Soft, non-distended, no focal peritoneal signs on initial exam.
Alert and oriented unless perfusion or metabolic derangement present.
Warm skin with fever; no purpura on initial survey.
054.clinical_tip: Petechiae + fever in a sick patient — treat empirically for meningococcemia.
054.objective: Recognize toxic rash syndromes. Resuscitate and cover infection.

interventions.label: Laboratory tests: Complete Blood Count (CBC), Coagulation studies, Liver function tests, Electrolytes, Glucose, Bilirubin levels (total and direct), Aminoaciduria screen, Urinalysis, Blood Type and Rh
Empiric Antibiotics IV
interventions.why: Do not delay for LP if unstable or petechial rash.

interventions.label: IV Fluid Bolus
interventions.why: Septic shock physiology common in toxic presentations.

interventions.label: Blood Cultures
interventions.why: Before antibiotics when possible — not if delaying care.

056.title: Abdominal Pain
056.diagnosis: Acute abdomen or intra-abdominal pathology (e.g., appendicitis, cholecystitis, mesenteric ischemia)
056.vitalsText: Initial Vital Signs Day 1 @ 18:00 Temperature: 37.2 C (98.9 degrees F) Pulse: 106 beat/min, Tachycardic Respiratory rate: 20 /minute Blood pressure, systolic: 114 mm Hg Blood pressure, diastolic: 79 mm Hg Height: 160.0 cm (63.0 in) Weight: 80.5 kg (177.4 lb) Body Mass Index: 31.4 kg/m2
exam: presents to the emergency room with abdominal pain, lethargy, and vomiting x2 days.
Abdominal tenderness pattern matches history; no rigid abdomen documented yet.
Alert and oriented unless perfusion or metabolic derangement present.
No acute rash; capillary refill and perfusion assessed.
056.clinical_tip: Elderly and immunocompromised may have minimal exam findings until rupture.
056.objective: Resuscitate, image, involve surgery early if indicated.

interventions.label: Obtain complete blood count (CBC), basic metabolic panel (BMP), liver function tests (LFTs), and coagulation studies
interventions.why: To assess for systemic inflammation, electrolyte imbalances, and potential complications such as peritonitis

interventions.label: Obtain abdominal X-ray, ultrasound, or computed tomography (CT) scan as indicated
interventions.why: To identify possible causes of acute abdominal pain and aid in diagnosing the underlying condition

interventions.label: Start intravenous fluid resuscitation
interventions.why: To maintain adequate hydration and blood pressure in the event of potential sepsis or peritonitis

interventions.label: Consider pain management with appropriate analgesics
interventions.why: To manage the patient's symptoms and improve their comfort

interventions.label: Consult general surgery if patient's condition deteriorates or if diagnostic workup is inconclusive
interventions.why: For further evaluation, management, and possible surgical intervention if necessary

interventions.label: CBC · Lipase · LFTs · UA
interventions.why: Narrow differential: biliary, pancreatic, UTI, medical.

interventions.label: CT Abdomen/Pelvis
interventions.why: High yield for appendicitis, diverticulitis, obstruction.

interventions.label: Antibiotics if indicated
interventions.why: Give before OR if sepsis or perforation suspected.

easy.intro: Initial History Reason(s) for visit: Abdominal Pain History of present Illness: A 22-year-old female presents to the emergency room with abdominal pain, lethargy, and vomiting x2 days. Her vomiting is non-bloody. Her abdominal pain is diffuse, which she rates as a 6/10. She also claims to have had an increased appetite prior to having these symptoms. She also claims that she’s been developing blurry vision, dizziness, and drowsiness. She also states that she is having increased urination Past Medical History Past Medical History: None Current medications: Birth control Allergies: None Vaccinations: The patient received her childhood vaccinations. She has never received any HPV vaccines. Women's health: The patient cannot remember when her last pap smear was done. Family History: The patient has a brother with Diabetes. Social History: Marital/Family: Single. She is an undergraduate student at a local university. Personal habits: The patient denies smoking. She drinks socially. She smoked marijuana "a few times" with her friends. She denies other illicit drugs or misuse of medications. 057.title: Abdominal Pain
057.vitalsText: Initial Vital Signs Day 1 @ 18:00 Temperature: 37.2 C (98.9 degrees F) Pulse: 106 beat/min, Tachycardic Respiratory rate: 20 /minute Blood pressure, systolic: 114 mm Hg Blood pressure, diastolic: 79 mm Hg Height: 160.0 cm (63.0 in) Weight: 80.5 kg (177.4 lb) Body Mass Index: 31.4 kg/m2
exam: presents to the emergency room with abdominal pain, lethargy, and vomiting x2 days.
Abdominal tenderness pattern matches history; no rigid abdomen documented yet.
Alert and oriented unless perfusion or metabolic derangement present.
No acute rash; capillary refill and perfusion assessed.
057.clinical_tip: Elderly and immunocompromised may have minimal exam findings until rupture.
057.objective: Resuscitate, image, involve surgery early if indicated.

interventions.label: Consider CT scan if symptoms persist or worsen
Consult General Surgery for possible exploratory laparotomy if symptoms suggest peritonitis, or Obstetrics and Gynecology if female of childbearing age with abdominal pain
Oxygen
interventions.why: To provide oxygen for the patient experiencing abdominal pain, which may be due to a variety of conditions including acute abdomen or peritonitis.

interventions.label: IV access ×2
interventions.why: To ensure adequate IV access in case the patient's condition worsens and requires fluid resuscitation or administration of medications.

interventions.label: glucose check
interventions.why: To evaluate for hypoglycemia, which can cause symptoms similar to abdominal pain.

058.title: Shortness of Breath
058.diagnosis: Pulmonary Embolism or Congestive Heart Failure (CHF) are common considerations in a patient presenting with Shortness of Breath of unknown etiology
exam: Dyspneic, speaking in short phrases if severe
058.clinical_tip: ABG or pulse ox + ECG + CXR in the first minutes.
058.objective: Stabilize hypoxia. Differentiate pulmonary vs cardiac vs metabolic.

interventions.label: Obtain vital signs and perform physical examination
interventions.why: To assess the patient's general condition and to guide further workup

interventions.label: Order electrocardiogram (ECG)
interventions.why: To detect heart abnormalities such as arrhythmias, ischemia or infarction that could be contributing to the symptoms

interventions.label: Order chest X-ray
interventions.why: To evaluate for pulmonary edema, consolidation, or pneumonia

interventions.label: Order D-dimer test
interventions.why: As it is a useful initial screening test for venous thromboembolism when clinical suspicion is high and can help guide further testing

interventions.label: If D-dimer is positive, order a ventilation/perfusion scan or computed tomography pulmonary angiogram (CTPA)

If the patient exhibits signs of heart failure such as jugular venous distention, peripheral edema, and crackles on lung auscultation, consider ordering a B-type natriuretic peptide (BNP) or N-terminal pro B-type natriuretic peptide (NT-proBNP)
Consider echocardiography if heart failure is suspected
interventions.why: To provide further information about cardiac function and structure

interventions.label: If the patient's condition deteriorates, consider intubation and mechanical ventilation
interventions.why: To support ventilation if the patient becomes hypoxic or dyspneic

interventions.label: Oxygen
interventions.why: Shortness of Breath is the chief complaint, making oxygen a necessary order immediately.

interventions.label: IV access ×2
interventions.why: The patient may require further interventions and IV access is essential for administering medications or fluids.

interventions.label: monitor
interventions.why: Vital signs monitoring is crucial to assess the patient's condition and response to initial interventions.

interventions.label: pulse ox
interventions.why: Pulse oximetry provides important information about the patient's oxygen saturation level.

easy.objective: Stabilize hypoxia. Differentiate pulmonary vs cardiac vs metabolic. Start with stabilization, then targeted testing.

exam: Dyspneic, speaking in short phrases if severe
059.clinical_tip: ABG or pulse ox + ECG + CXR in the first minutes.

interventions.why: The patient is experiencing shortness of breath, indicating potential respiratory distress. Oxygen is the first life-saving intervention that must be ordered immediately in this case.

interventions.label: Initial resuscitation: IV fluid, oxygen support, pain management
Laboratory tests: CBC, metabolic panel, lipase, amylase
Imaging: Abdominal CT scan (non-contrast initially, contrast if no perforation is suspected)
Diagnosis may require exploratory laparotomy or laparoscopy
interventions.why: If other tests fail to establish a diagnosis, surgical exploration may be required

interventions.label: Oxygen
interventions.why: Abdominal pain can be a symptom of several life-threatening conditions. Oxygen is crucial to ensure adequate oxygenation and stabilize the patient.

interventions.label: Order: Order: time-sensitive interventions first; (oxygen, IV access, glucose check)
interventions.why: Vital signs monitoring, oxygen administration, and establishing IV access are critical initial steps in any emergency situation.

061.title: Headache
061.diagnosis: Migraine or other secondary causes of headache (such as subarachnoid hemorrhage, meningitis, or temporal arteritis) are possible
061.vitalsText: Initial Vital Signs Day 1 @ 9:00 Temperature: 38.3 C (101.0 degrees F) Pulse: 88 beats/min. Regular rhythm Respiratory rate: 18 /minute Blood pressure, systolic: 118 mm Hg Blood pressure, diastolic: 70 mm Hg Height: 175.3 cm (69.0 in) Weight: 65.8 kg (145 lb) Body Mass Index: 21.4 kg/m2
exam: presents to your office with a an occipital headache, mild vertigo, and a tendency to fall when walking for 6 days.
Abdominal tenderness pattern matches history; no rigid abdomen documented yet.
Mental status intact; cranial nerves and neck stiffness assessed.
Skin rash morphology and distribution documented.
061.clinical_tip: Thunderclap headache = SAH workup. Do not send home without considering red flags.
061.objective: Rule out SAH, meningitis, mass, and hypertensive emergency.

interventions.label: Obtain immediate imaging (CT or MRI) to rule out any acute intracranial pathology
Order a lumbar puncture for CSF analysis if imaging is normal or inconclusive
order1
interventions.why: The order should be avoided because it is incomplete and lacks specific details such as patient information, treatment indication, and clinical reasoning.

interventions.label: Oxygen
interventions.why: The patient is experiencing a headache, which could be a symptom of various life-threatening conditions. Oxygen is crucial to ensure adequate oxygenation.

interventions.label: IV access x2 large-bore
interventions.why: In emergency situations like this, having large-bore IV access ensures rapid administration of fluids and medications.

easy.intro: Initial History Reason(s) for visit: Headache History of present Illness: A 55-year-old female with a past medical history of hypertension presents to your office with a an occipital headache, mild vertigo, and a tendency to fall when walking for 6 days. She states that she never had any problems walking or being off-balance. Eight days ago, she stated that she had a pink rash on her arms and torso and felt feverish. The rash improved after a couple days, but the headache and tendency to fall persisted. She had nausea, but she denied any vomiting. She feels weak and "under the weather." She denies travel, but went hiking 2 weeks ago nearby her house which she routinely does every summer. She denies any history of migraines. She will occasionally get a tension headache which she will treat with Ibuprofen. She has never had a headache that has lasted this long. She denies any trauma to her head. She denies any sick contacts. She denies any recent antibiotic use. She is very concerned about her symptoms. PMH: Past Medical History: Hypertension. The patient recently had a colonoscopy screening which was normal. Current medications: HCTZ 25mg PO daily Allergies: None Vaccinations: Received Childhood vaccinations. Women's health: The patient cannot remember when her last pap smear was done. Family History: Her father has a history of Hypertension and died of a stroke. Her mother has no health problems and is still living. Social History: Marital/Family: She is married with one child. She lives in Gaithersburg, Maryland. Personal habits: The patient denies smoking. She lives an active lifestyle. She drinks alcohol socially. She denies use of illegal drugs or misuse of medications. Occupation/educational: She works as a public relations specialist. She is a college graduate with a BS in communication. Recreational: The patient enjoys to bike and hike. She loves the outdoors and spends as much time as she is able. 062.title: Altered Mental Status
062.vitalsText: Initial Vital Signs Day 1 @ 11:00 Temperature: 36.8 C (98.2 degrees F) Pulse: 88 beats/min. Regular rhythm Respiratory rate: 18 /minute Blood pressure, systolic: 115 mm Hg Blood pressure, diastolic: 85 mm Hg Height: 180cm (70.9 in) Weight: 91.0 kg (200.6 lb) Body Mass Index: 27.9 kg/m2
exam: Decreased engagement and cognitive change compared with reported baseline.
Soft, non-distended, no focal peritoneal signs on initial exam.
Altered mental status with attention and command-following documented.
No acute rash; capillary refill and perfusion assessed.
062.clinical_tip: Fingerstick glucose before CT — hypoglycemia mimics stroke.

interventions.label: Obtain Stat labs including CBC, Electrolytes, Glucose, BUN, Creatinine, Liver Function Tests, Coagulation studies, Toxicology screen, and Blood cultures.
interventions.why: To identify potential causes of altered mental status such as electrolyte imbalances, metabolic disorders, infections, or drug intoxications.

interventions.label: Immediate CT Head without contrast for acute changes.
Oxygen
interventions.why: The patient presents with altered mental status, which may indicate hypoxia. Oxygen is essential to address this potential life-threatening condition.

interventions.label: IV access ×2 large-bore
interventions.why: Large-bore IV access is crucial for administering fluids and medications quickly in an emergency situation like this.

easy.intro: Initial History Reason(s) for visit: Altered mental status History of present Illness: A 70-year-old Caucasian man comes to the Emergency room because his family members are concerned that something is wrong. The patient has been having behavioral changes over a period of three or four weeks. He used to be very active and managed all of his financial affairs. He now barely talks, stares at the wall for hours at a time, and sleeps most of the day. Four weeks ago, the patient got up to urinate and fell before reaching the bathroom. He was found on the floor the next morning by family members. He was taken to his family doctor and was discharged with a prescription to buy a cane. Since that time, he has been having problems walking. There have been no fevers or night sweats. The patient is currently in no pain. Past Medical History: Chronic alcohol abuse; cataracts. Hospitalization Procedures: Hemorrhoidectomy 1 year ago. Last colonoscopy was 4 years ago which demonstrated no abnormalities. Current medications: Aspirin 81mg PO daily Allergies: None Vaccinations: Up to date Family History: Father: Deceased due to a 'heart attack'. Mother: Diagnosed with type 2 diabetes mellitus and died of natural causes. The patient has no siblings. Social History: Marital/Family: Widowed; he has three children. Personal habits: the patient smokes 1 Pack/day. He currently does not drink alcohol but was a chronic alcoholic for 30 years. Denies use of illegal drugs or misuse of medications. Occupation/educational: He is a retired Army human resources manager. He is a high school graduate. Recreational: The patient likes to spend time with his children and enjoys collecting stamps. I was found on the floor the next morning by family members. I was taken to his family doctor and was discharged with a prescription to buy a cane. Since that time, he has been having problems walking. There have been no fevers or night sweats. I is currently in no pain. Past Medical History: Chronic alcohol abuse; cataracts. Hospitalization Procedures: Hemorrhoidectomy 1 year ago. Last colonoscopy was 4 years ago which demonstrated no abnormalities. Current medications: Aspirin 81mg PO daily Allergies: None Vaccinations: Up to date Family History: Father: Deceased due to a 'heart attack'. Mother: Diagnosed with type 2 diabetes mellitus and died of natural causes. I has no sibl

063.title: Malaise
063.diagnosis: Malaise can be a symptom of various underlying conditions such as infections, autoimmune disorders, malignancies, or metabolic diseases. Until a specific diagnosis is made, it's essential to consider broad-spectrum diagnoses.
exam: Malaise — exam tailored to chief complaint on arrival.
Soft, non-distended, no focal peritoneal signs on initial exam.
Alert and oriented unless perfusion or metabolic derangement present.
No acute rash; capillary refill and perfusion assessed.

interventions.label: Consider imaging studies such as chest X-ray or CT scan if indicated
interventions.why: To identify possible infections, masses, or other pathologies

interventions.label: Consider infectious workup including HIV testing, Lyme disease serology, and urine/blood cultures

interventions.why: For further evaluation and potential management of the underlying condition

interventions.label: Oxygen
interventions.why: The patient is experiencing malaise, a symptom that may indicate distress or acute illness. Oxygen administration is crucial for maintaining adequate oxygenation.

interventions.label: IV access ×2
interventions.why: Intravenous access is essential for administering fluids, medications, and blood products as needed.

064.title: Abdominal Pain
064.vitalsText: Initial Vital Signs Day 1 @ 18:00 Temperature: 37.2 C (98.9 degrees F) Pulse: 106 beat/min, Tachycardic Respiratory rate: 20 /minute Blood pressure, systolic: 114 mm Hg Blood pressure, diastolic: 79 mm Hg Height: 160.0 cm (63.0 in) Weight: 80.5 kg (177.4 lb) Body Mass Index: 31.4 kg/m2
exam: presents to the emergency room with abdominal pain, lethargy, and vomiting x2 days.
Abdominal tenderness pattern matches history; no rigid abdomen documented yet.
Alert and oriented unless perfusion or metabolic derangement present.
No acute rash; capillary refill and perfusion assessed.
064.clinical_tip: Elderly and immunocompromised may have minimal exam findings until rupture.
064.objective: Resuscitate, image, involve surgery early if indicated.

interventions.label: Obtain complete blood count (CBC)
interventions.why: To assess for anemia, infection, or leukocytosis

interventions.label: Obtain basic metabolic panel (BMP)
interventions.why: To evaluate electrolyte levels and kidney function

interventions.label: Obtain urinalysis
interventions.why: To rule out urinary tract infections

interventions.label: Obtain abdominal X-ray
interventions.why: To assess for free air, bowel obstruction or pneumoperitoneum

interventions.label: Consider obtaining ultrasound or CT scan of the abdomen
interventions.why: For further imaging to help determine the cause of the pain

interventions.label: If fever is present, obtain C-reactive protein (CRP) and white blood cell count (WBC)
Consult with a general surgery specialist if symptoms suggest peritonitis or free air in the abdomen
IV fluids
interventions.why: For hydration due to abdominal pain

easy.intro: Initial History Reason(s) for visit: Abdominal Pain History of present Illness: A 22-year-old female presents to the emergency room with abdominal pain, lethargy, and vomiting x2 days. Her vomiting is non-bloody. Her abdominal pain is diffuse, which she rates as a 6/10. She also claims to have had an increased appetite prior to having these symptoms. She also claims that she’s been developing blurry vision, dizziness, and drowsiness. She also states that she is having increased urination Past Medical History Past Medical History: None Current medications: Birth control Allergies: None Vaccinations: The patient received her childhood vaccinations. She has never received any HPV vaccines. Women's health: The patient cannot remember when her last pap smear was done. Family History: The patient has a brother with Diabetes. Social History: Marital/Family: Single. She is an undergraduate student at a local university. Personal habits: The patient denies smoking. She drinks socially. She smoked marijuana "a few times" with her friends. She denies other illicit drugs or misuse of medications. Occupation/educational: She is a student at a local university Recreational: She plays the piano and sings in a band. Review of systems: General: See HPI Skin: Her skin is dry. interventions.label: Order laboratory tests (CBC, Chemistry panel, Thyroid function tests) to rule out any medical causes of the patient's symptoms
Oxygen
interventions.why: Anxiety could potentially be a sign of underlying distress or AMS, requiring oxygen immediately.

interventions.label: IV access ×2
interventions.why: In case further interventions are needed, it's essential to have adequate IV access.

066.title: Cough
066.diagnosis: Possible Lower Respiratory Tract Infection (LRTI) or Asthma exacerbation due to chief complaint being cough
exam: Cough — exam tailored to chief complaint on arrival.
Soft, non-distended, no focal peritoneal signs on initial exam.
Alert and oriented unless perfusion or metabolic derangement present.
No acute rash; capillary refill and perfusion assessed.
066.clinical_tip: Until proven otherwise, treat unstable chest pain as ACS.
066.objective: Rule out life threats. ECG within 10 minutes.

interventions.label: Order Complete Blood Count (CBC), Blood Chemistry, and Inflammatory Markers (CRP, ESR)
Inhaled bronchodilators trial for symptomatic relief if patient has a history of asthma or respiratory symptoms suggestive of asthma
Consult Respiratory Medicine if symptoms persist or worsen, or if there is suspicion of pneumonia or other complex lung conditions
Arrange follow-up appointment for re-evaluation and adjustment of treatment plan
Oxygen
interventions.why: Cough can be a sign of respiratory distress, necessitating immediate oxygen delivery. IV access is required for medication administration and fluid resuscitation if necessary.

interventions.label: IV access ×2
interventions.why: Large-bore IV access provides quick and efficient fluid resuscitation in case of hypovolemia or shock.

interventions.label: Nasal cannula
interventions.why: Nasal cannula is typically the initial oxygen delivery method for respiratory distress.

068.title: Muscle Weakness
068.diagnosis: Neuromuscular Disorder (NMD) - such as Guillain-Barre Syndrome, Myasthenia Gravis, or Motor Neuron Disease, considering the symptoms of muscle weakness
exam: Muscle Weakness — exam tailored to chief complaint on arrival.
Soft, non-distended, no focal peritoneal signs on initial exam.
Motor strength and reflexes tested for fatigability and focal deficits.
No acute rash; capillary refill and perfusion assessed.
068.clinical_tip: Weakness + hyperreflexia vs hyporeflexia localizes — think electrolytes and cord.
068.objective: Differentiate metabolic, neurologic, and toxic causes.

interventions.label: Lumbar Puncture for CSF Analysis
interventions.why: To evaluate the cerebrospinal fluid (CSF) for protein levels and cell counts, which may help in diagnosing Guillain-Barre Syndrome

interventions.label: Electromyography (EMG) and Nerve Conduction Studies (NCS)
interventions.why: To assess the electrical activity of muscles and nerves, which is useful in identifying nerve damage or disorders

interventions.label: Thyroid Function Tests (TFTs) to rule out myxedema
Acetylcholine Receptor Antibody Test to evaluate Myasthenia Gravis
Consult Neurology
interventions.why: For further evaluation and management of the patient's neurological condition

interventions.label: MRI Spine if cord signs
interventions.why: Cord compression is a surgical emergency.

interventions.label: Admit / Neuro consult
interventions.why: Progressive weakness or respiratory concern → ICU.

easy.clinicalTip: Teaching hint: Weakness + hyperreflexia vs hyporeflexia localizes — think electrolytes and cord.
easy.objective: Differentiate metabolic, neurologic, and toxic causes. Start with stabilization, then targeted testing.

070.title: Fever
070.diagnosis: Infectious workup including sepsis, meningitis, or pneumonia are potential considerations due to the fever being of unknown origin.
exam: Fever — exam tailored to chief complaint on arrival.
Soft, non-distended, no focal peritoneal signs on initial exam.
Alert and oriented unless perfusion or metabolic derangement present.
Warm skin with fever; no purpura on initial survey.
070.clinical_tip: Petechiae + fever in a sick patient — treat empirically for meningococcemia.
070.objective: Recognize toxic rash syndromes. Resuscitate and cover infection.

interventions.label: Obtain blood cultures before starting antibiotics
Perform complete blood count (CBC), comprehensive metabolic panel (CMP), and urinalysis
Consider obtaining lumbar puncture (LP) for cerebrospinal fluid analysis in cases where meningitis is suspected
Obtain a chest X-ray or consider computed tomography (CT) scan if pneumonia is suspected
Start empiric broad-spectrum antibiotics after obtaining blood cultures
Oxygen
interventions.why: Fever is a symptom that could indicate an infection, and the patient may be in distress. Oxygen should be administered immediately.

interventions.label: IV access ×2

interventions.label: Complete Blood Count (CBC)
interventions.why: To evaluate the patient's overall health and detect potential issues with red blood cells, white blood cells, and platelets.

interventions.label: Chemistry Panel
interventions.why: To assess kidney function, liver function, electrolyte levels, and glucose levels.

interventions.label: Urinalysis
interventions.why: To evaluate kidney function, detect urinary tract infections or other abnormalities.

interventions.label: Electrocardiogram (ECG)
interventions.why: To evaluate the patient's heart rhythm and cardiac health.

interventions.label: Imaging studies (X-ray, CT, MRI as necessary)
interventions.why: To visualize internal organs and structures for potential abnormalities.

interventions.label: Universal Labs (CBC, CMP, UA)
interventions.why: Establishing baseline renal, liver, and electrolyte functions, as well as urinalysis results is essential for a comprehensive workup.

interventions.label: EKG + CXR if indicated
interventions.why: An EKG can help in detecting cardiac abnormalities while a chest X-ray can aid in identifying lung pathologies.

073.title: Memory Loss
073.diagnosis: Dementia (e.g., Alzheimer's disease, vascular dementia, Lewy body dementia, frontotemporal dementia)
exam: Family reports progressive memory loss and personality change
Impaired short-term recall; disorientation; gait instability with recent falls
No meningismus; assess for bruising from falls
073.clinical_tip: Separate acute delirium from progressive dementia — always rule out reversible metabolic, infectious, and structural causes first.
073.objective: Stabilize, evaluate reversible causes of cognitive decline, and image when indicated.

interventions.label: Laboratory workup: Complete blood count, Chemistry panel, Thyroid function tests, Vitamin B12 and Folate levels
Imaging: MRI brain with and without contrast
Neurology consultation for further evaluation and management
Psychiatry consultation for assessment of behavioral symptoms and psychological support
Oxygen
interventions.why: Memory loss can be a sign of hypoxia or impending respiratory failure.

interventions.label: Order: Order: time-sensitive interventions first; (oxygen, IV access, glucose check)
interventions.why: Stabilizing the patient's airway, breathing, and circulation should be prioritized in emergency situations.

075.title: Chest Pain
075.diagnosis: Acute Coronary Syndrome (ACS) or Aortic Dissection is a common differential diagnosis for an unknown cause of chest pain.
075.vitalsText: Initial Vital Signs Day 1 @ 16:00 Temperature: 37.0 C (98.6 degrees F) Pulse: 120 beats/min, Weak Respiratory rate: 34 /minute Blood pressure, systolic: 100 mm Hg Blood pressure, diastolic: 60 mm Hg Height: 183cm (72.0 in) Weight: 97.5 kg (215 lb) Body Mass Index: 29.1 kg/m2
exam: Chest Pain — exam tailored to chief complaint on arrival.
Soft, non-distended, no focal peritoneal signs on initial exam.
Alert and oriented unless perfusion or metabolic derangement present.
No acute rash; capillary refill and perfusion assessed.
075.clinical_tip: Until proven otherwise, treat unstable chest pain as ACS.
075.objective: Rule out life threats. ECG within 10 minutes.

interventions.label: Electrocardiogram (ECG)
interventions.why: To evaluate for signs of ischemia or myocardial infarction.

interventions.label: Troponin I/T levels
interventions.why: For the detection and evaluation of myocardial damage.

interventions.label: Chest X-ray
interventions.why: To evaluate for alternative causes of chest pain, such as pneumonia or pulmonary embolism.

interventions.label: Consult Cardiology
interventions.why: To discuss further diagnostic tests, potential treatments, and management strategies.

interventions.label: Pain management with aspirin and nitroglycerin
interventions.why: Temporary pain relief and potential benefits in reducing ischemia.

interventions.label: Obtain a thorough medical history and perform a physical examination
order2
interventions.why: appropriate medication and dose based on guidelines

interventions.label: Oxygen
interventions.why: Chest pain can be a symptom of various life-threatening conditions such as myocardial infarction or pulmonary embolism. Immediate oxygen administration is crucial to stabilize the patient.

interventions.label: Order: Order: time-sensitive interventions first; (oxygen, IV access, glucose check)
interventions.why: Stabilizing the patient's vital signs and checking for hypoglycemia are critical initial steps in managing chest pain.

easy.intro: Initial History Reason(s) for visit: Chest pain; respiratory distress History of present Illness: The patient, a 65-year-old accountant, is brought to the emergency department by ambulance from the trucking company, where he works. Ten minutes prior to the ambulance’s arrival, the patient developed excruciating, sharp pain on the right side of his chest accompanied by marked respiratory distress. He rates the pain as an 8 on a 10-point scale. The pain increases on respiration, and he is unable to answer questions. A coworker who accompanied the patient to the hospital says that this has never happened to the patient in the past. Coworker mentions patient has a past medical history of emphysema and asthma. Oxygen was administered during transport.

interventions.why: To help identify the specific causative virus

interventions.label: Oxygen
interventions.why: The patient is experiencing fever and rash, which could be indicative of a systemic infection. Oxygen should be administered to maintain adequate oxygenation.

interventions.label: IV access ×2
interventions.why: The case mentions severe distress or altered mental status (AMS), so it's important to secure IV access for fluid resuscitation and medication administration.

interventions.label: Glucose check
interventions.why: Hypoglycemia can cause symptoms similar to fever and rash. A quick glucose check is essential in this scenario.

077.vitalsText: Initial Vital Signs Day 1 @ 16:00 Temperature: 37.0 C (98.6 degrees F) Pulse: 120 beats/min, Weak Respiratory rate: 34 /minute Blood pressure, systolic: 100 mm Hg Blood pressure, diastolic: 60 mm Hg Height: 183cm (72.0 in) Weight: 97.5 kg (215 lb) Body Mass Index: 29.1 kg/m2

interventions.label: EKG - to evaluate for electrocardiographic signs of ischemia
Troponin I or Troponin T labs - for cardiac muscle damage evaluation
Chest X-ray - to assess for pulmonary causes and cardiac silhouette
D-dimer lab - for possible pulmonary embolism
CT Angiography or Echocardiogram - for assessment of coronary arteries, aorta, and potential thrombus
Pulmonary Function Tests (if needed based on chest X-ray results)
Oxygen
interventions.why: Chest pain can indicate several life-threatening conditions, and early oxygen administration is crucial.

interventions.label: IV access ×2
interventions.why: Large-bore IV access allows for rapid fluid resuscitation if needed.

interventions.label: glucose check
interventions.why: Hypoglycemia can present with symptoms similar to chest pain.

078.title: Generalized Weakness
078.diagnosis: The most likely diagnoses could include Guillain-Barré Syndrome, Myasthenia Gravis, Lambert-Eaton Myasthenic Syndrome, or other neuromuscular disorders.
078.vitalsText: Initial Vital Signs Day 1 @ 9:00 Temperature: 37.0 C (98.6 degrees F) Pulse: 110 beat/min, Regular rhythm; bounding Respiratory rate: 18 /minute Blood pressure, systolic: 122 mm Hg Blood pressure, diastolic: 74 mm Hg Height: 175.3 cm (69.0 in) Weight: 65.8 kg (145 lb) Body Mass Index: 21.4 kg/m2
exam: presents to your office complaining of progressive generalized weakness that has been occurring for the past 3 months.
Soft, non-distended, no focal peritoneal signs on initial exam.
Motor strength and reflexes tested for fatigability and focal deficits.
Skin rash morphology and distribution documented.
078.clinical_tip: Weakness + hyperreflexia vs hyporeflexia localizes — think electrolytes and cord.
078.objective: Differentiate metabolic, neurologic, and toxic causes.

interventions.label: Obtain urgent laboratory workup including CBC, Chemistry panel, LFTs, Coagulation profile, TSH, T4, B12, Folic acid, ESR, CRP, and Rheumatoid factor
Consider ordering a CSF analysis for protein levels if there is suspicion of a demyelinating disorder such as Guillain-Barré Syndrome
Order nerve conduction studies (NCS) and electromyography (EMG) to evaluate the electrical activity of muscles and nerves
Consider ordering a chest X-ray or CT scan if there is suspicion of an underlying infection or malignancy
Consult Neurology for further evaluation and management
MRI Spine if cord signs
interventions.why: Cord compression is a surgical emergency.

interventions.label: Admit / Neuro consult
interventions.why: Progressive weakness or respiratory concern → ICU.

easy.intro: Initial History Reason(s) for visit: Generalized weakness History of present Illness: A previously healthy 30-year-old female with a PMH of asthma presents to your office complaining of progressive generalized weakness that has been occurring for the past 3 months. Her symptoms worsen at the end of the day, and her weakness improves with rest. She reports occasional blurry vision with improvement after closing and resting her eyes. She also notes difficulty chewing food and swallowing recently. She has also been experiencing dyspnea with strenuous exercise, but it is now refractory to her albuterol inhaler. Past Medical History: She broke her arm when she was 14 which required an open reduction and pin placement. Current medications: Birth control, multi-vitamin Allergies: Penicillin (rash and shortness of breath) Vaccinations: Received Childhood vaccinations. Women's health: She contracted gonorrhea from a sexual partner at age 20 which was successfully treated. She had a LEEP procedure done due to an abnormal pap smear 6 years ago. Her last pap smear was done 3 years ago which was normal. Family History: Her father has a history of congestive heart failure and is status post two myocardial infarctions. Her mother has hypothyroidism. Her grandfather died of a stroke. Social History: Marital/Family: The patient is single. She is currently living and is sexually active with her boyfriend of 2 years. Personal habits: She states that she drinks occasionally. The patient smokes cigars with her boyfriend on occasion when going out. Occupation/educational: She works as a newspaper editor for a local newspaper. Recreational: She enjoys reading and breeds dogs. Review of systems: General: See HPI Skin: Negative HEENT: See HPI Cardiorespiratory: Negative Gastrointestinal: Negative Genitourinary: Negative Neuropsychiatric: See HPI

079.title: Back Pain
079.diagnosis: Degenerative Disc Disease, Spinal Stenosis, or Herniated Disc - Initial Presentation of Back Pain
exam: Back Pain — exam tailored to chief complaint on arrival.
Soft, non-distended, no focal peritoneal signs on initial exam.
Alert and oriented unless perfusion or metabolic derangement present.
No acute rash; capillary refill and perfusion assessed.
079.clinical_tip: Weakness + hyperreflexia vs hyporeflexia localizes — think electrolytes and cord.
079.objective: Differentiate metabolic, neurologic, and toxic causes.

interventions.label: Order imaging studies (MRI or CT scan) to determine the specific cause and location of back pain
Refer the patient to a Neurologist or Orthopedic Surgeon for further evaluation and management
CMP/BMP
interventions.why: Evaluate electrolytes, renal, and liver function

interventions.label: Chest X-ray
interventions.why: Potential spinal cord injury or trauma

exam: Vomiting — exam tailored to chief complaint on arrival.

Oxygen
interventions.why: The patient is experiencing vomiting, which could be a symptom of various conditions. Oxygen should be provided immediately due to the potential for respiratory compromise.

interventions.label: IV access ×2
interventions.why: IV access is crucial for administration of necessary fluids and medications. Two large-bore IVs are ordered to ensure adequate access in case of rapid fluid resuscitation.

081.title: Diarrhea

exam: Diarrhea — exam tailored to chief complaint on arrival.
Soft, non-distended, no focal peritoneal signs on initial exam.
Alert and oriented unless perfusion or metabolic derangement present.
No acute rash; capillary refill and perfusion assessed.
081.clinical_tip: Elderly and immunocompromised may have minimal exam findings until rupture.

interventions.label: Obtain stool ova and parasites examination
interventions.why: For evaluation of possible parasitic infection

interventions.label: Oxygen
interventions.why: The patient is experiencing diarrhea, which can lead to dehydration and electrolyte imbalance. Oxygen is essential for maintaining adequate oxygen saturation.

interventions.label: IV access x2
interventions.why: The patient may require fluid resuscitation due to potential dehydration caused by prolonged diarrhea. Large-bore IV access allows for efficient administration of fluids.

interventions.label: Follow-up Actions (Next 30 Seconds)

interventions.label: Order electrocardiogram (ECG) for baseline cardiac evaluation
Obtain abdominal and pelvic imaging (ultrasound or CT scan, depending on local protocols and patient's condition)
Monitor vital signs, pain level, and physical exam findings
Oxygen
interventions.why: The patient is experiencing abdominal pain which could be a sign of acute abdomen or other life-threatening conditions. Immediate oxygen administration is crucial in such cases.

interventions.label: Order: Order: time-sensitive interventions first; (oxygen, IV access, glucose check)
interventions.why: This order ensures immediate stabilization of the patient by providing oxygen, establishing IV access, and checking blood glucose level.

easy.intro: Initial History Reason(s) for visit: Abdominal Pain History of present Illness: A 22-year-old female presents to the emergency room with abdominal pain, lethargy, and vomiting x2 days. Her vomiting is non-bloody. Her abdominal pain is diffuse, which she rates as a 6/10. She also claims to have had an increased appetite prior to having these symptoms. She also claims that she’s been developing blurry vision, dizziness, and drowsiness. She also states that she is having increased urination Past Medical History Past Medical History: None Current medications: Birth control Allergies: None Vaccinations: The patient received her childhood vaccinations. She has never received any HPV vaccines. Women's health: The patient cannot remember when her last pap smear was done. Family History: The patient has a brother with Diabetes. Social History: Marital/Family: Single. She is an undergraduate student at a local university. Personal habits: The patient denies smoking. She drinks socially. She smoked marijuana "a few times" with her friends. She denies other illicit drugs or misuse of medications. Occupation/educational: She is a student at a local university Recreational: She plays the piano and sings in a band. Review of systems: General: See HPI Skin: Her skin is dry. HEENT: Her throat is dry. Cardiorespiratory: She feels that her heart is beating faster. interventions.why: Give before OR if sepsis or perforation suspected.

084.title: Abdominal Pain
084.diagnosis: Abdominal pain of unknown origin, potential causes include appendicitis, cholecystitis, mesenteric ischemia, peptic ulcer disease, pancreatitis, and gastrointestinal bleeding.
084.vitalsText: Initial Vital Signs Day 1 @ 18:00 Temperature: 37.2 C (98.9 degrees F) Pulse: 106 beat/min, Tachycardic Respiratory rate: 20 /minute Blood pressure, systolic: 114 mm Hg Blood pressure, diastolic: 79 mm Hg Height: 160.0 cm (63.0 in) Weight: 80.5 kg (177.4 lb) Body Mass Index: 31.4 kg/m2
exam: presents to the emergency room with abdominal pain, lethargy, and vomiting x2 days.
Abdominal tenderness pattern matches history; no rigid abdomen documented yet.
Alert and oriented unless perfusion or metabolic derangement present.
No acute rash; capillary refill and perfusion assessed.
084.clinical_tip: Elderly and immunocompromised may have minimal exam findings until rupture.
084.objective: Resuscitate, image, involve surgery early if indicated.

interventions.label: Obtain laboratory studies: CBC, metabolic panel, lipase, amylase
Imaging: Consider CT scan of abdomen and pelvis with contrast if not contraindicated
Oxygen
interventions.why: The patient is experiencing abdominal pain, which may indicate a serious condition requiring immediate oxygen support.

interventions.label: IV access ×2
interventions.why: Large-bore IV access is essential for administering fluids and potential medications in case of an emergency.

interventions.label: Nasal cannula
interventions.why: As the patient's condition is unknown, nasal cannula is a standard initial intervention for providing oxygen support.

interventions.label: CMP/BMP
interventions.why: CMP (Comprehensive Metabolic Panel) and BMP (Basic Metabolic Panel) are essential to evaluate renal and liver function, which may be affected by various causes of abdominal pain.

088.title: Frequency of Urination
088.diagnosis: Unknown frequency of urination may suggest Lower Urinary Tract Symptoms (LUTS) or Benign Prostatic Hyperplasia (BPH) in males, and Urinary Tract Infection (UTI), Overactive Bladder (OAB), Diabetes Mellitus or Chronic Kidney Disease in all patients. However, further evaluation is needed.
exam: Frequency of Urination — exam tailored to chief complaint on arrival.
Soft, non-distended, no focal peritoneal signs on initial exam.
Alert and oriented unless perfusion or metabolic derangement present.
Warm skin with fever; no purpura on initial survey.
088.clinical_tip: Complicated UTI in men, pregnancy, or fever → admit and image.
088.objective: Treat infection; rule out pyelonephritis and STI.

interventions.label: Obtain urinalysis to rule out urinary tract infection.
Order a renal function panel to evaluate kidney function and potential diabetes.
CMP/BMP
interventions.why: Electrolytes and renal/liver status are important for a variety of conditions, including kidney problems which can cause frequent urination. Urinalysis ± culture is necessary to identify potential infections that may cause symptoms.

089.title: Burns to Face and Buttocks
089.diagnosis: The most likely diagnoses for burns to face and buttocks of unknown etiology could be thermal burns, chemical burns, or electrical burns. However, it's important to note that the diagnosis may vary based on the specific presentation, history, and examination findings.
exam: Burns to Face and Buttocks — exam tailored to chief complaint on arrival.
Soft, non-distended, no focal peritoneal signs on initial exam.
Alert and oriented unless perfusion or metabolic derangement present.
No acute rash; capillary refill and perfusion assessed.
089.clinical_tip: Stabilize first — ABCs, IV access, monitor, then targeted workup.
089.objective: Identify and treat life threats within the time limit.

interventions.label: Perform a thorough physical exam with particular attention to the extent and depth of the burns, presence of inhalation injury, and any systemic symptoms
Send blood for complete blood count (CBC), electrolytes, coagulation profile, and arterial blood gas if needed
Obtain a urine toxicology screen if chemical burn is suspected
Administer appropriate pain management and fluid resuscitation per burn wound surface area and patient's hemodynamic status
Apply cool water to the burns for initial relief, then cover with clean, dry, non-adherent dressings
Consult Plastic Surgery, Burn Center, or Emergency Medicine specialists as appropriate for further management and potential escharotomy if needed
Prepare for possible airway management and intubation if the burn involves the face or inhalation injury is suspected
Oxygen
interventions.why: The patient has burns to the face and buttocks, which can cause severe distress or acute medical symptoms (AMS), requiring immediate oxygen.

interventions.label: IV access ×2 large-bore
interventions.why: Large-bore IV access is necessary for rapid fluid resuscitation due to the extensive nature of the burn injuries.

090.title: Prenatal Followup
090.diagnosis: The most likely diagnoses for an unknown prenatal follow-up could include various pregnancy complications, fetal anomalies, or maternal conditions. However, it's important to note that without specific symptoms or findings, a definitive diagnosis cannot be made.
exam: Prenatal Followup — exam tailored to chief complaint on arrival.
Soft, non-distended, no focal peritoneal signs on initial exam.
Alert and oriented unless perfusion or metabolic derangement present.
No acute rash; capillary refill and perfusion assessed.
090.clinical_tip: Positive pregnancy test changes everything — ectopic until proven otherwise.
090.objective: Rule out ectopic, torsion, and rupture.

interventions.label: Maternal lab work (CBC, glucose screen, urinalysis, etc.)
Oxygen
interventions.why: The patient is pregnant, and supplemental oxygen should be administered in all cases of prenatal followup due to potential fetal hypoxia.

interventions.label: IV access ×2
interventions.why: Large-bore IV access is necessary for administering fluids and medications during labor and delivery.

interventions.label: Pulse oximetry
interventions.why: Monitoring the patient's oxygen saturation levels is essential in prenatal care.

interventions.label: Chest X-ray
interventions.why: Although there are no clear indications of cardiopulmonary issues, a chest X-ray may be useful to rule out potential complications during pregnancy.

easy.clinicalTip: Teaching hint: Positive pregnancy test changes everything — ectopic until proven otherwise.
easy.objective: Rule out ectopic, torsion, and rupture. Start with stabilization, then targeted testing.

standard.objective: Rule out ectopic, torsion, and rupture.

092.title: Vaginal Bleeding
092.diagnosis: Vaginal bleeding could be due to various causes such as endometrial cancer, cervical cancer, uterine fibroids, vaginitis, or trauma. Given the uncertainty of diagnosis, a comprehensive workup is required.
exam: Vaginal Bleeding — exam tailored to chief complaint on arrival.
Soft, non-distended, no focal peritoneal signs on initial exam.
Alert and oriented unless perfusion or metabolic derangement present.
No acute rash; capillary refill and perfusion assessed.
092.clinical_tip: Positive pregnancy test changes everything — ectopic until proven otherwise.
092.objective: Rule out ectopic, torsion, and rupture.

interventions.label: Laboratory tests: Complete blood count (CBC), coagulation panel, urinalysis, and pregnancy test
Imaging studies: Transvaginal ultrasound, pelvic MRI (if available)
Oxygen
interventions.why: Vaginal bleeding can be a symptom of various conditions, including pregnancy. Oxygen and IV access are essential life-saving interventions.

interventions.label: IV access ×2 large-bore
interventions.why: For potential blood loss assessment and fluid resuscitation if necessary.

interventions.label: pregnancy test (β-hCG)
interventions.why: Vaginal bleeding is a common symptom in pregnancy. A β-hCG test will help determine the stage of pregnancy or possible ectopic pregnancy.

093.title: Altered Mental Status
093.diagnosis: Altered Mental Status - Acute brain disorder, likely of infectious, metabolic, toxicological, or structural etiology
093.vitalsText: Initial Vital Signs Day 1 @ 11:00 Temperature: 36.8 C (98.2 degrees F) Pulse: 88 beats/min. Regular rhythm Respiratory rate: 18 /minute Blood pressure, systolic: 115 mm Hg Blood pressure, diastolic: 85 mm Hg Height: 180cm (70.9 in) Weight: 91.0 kg (200.6 lb) Body Mass Index: 27.9 kg/m2
exam: Decreased engagement and cognitive change compared with reported baseline.
Soft, non-distended, no focal peritoneal signs on initial exam.
Altered mental status with attention and command-following documented.
No acute rash; capillary refill and perfusion assessed.
093.clinical_tip: Fingerstick glucose before CT — hypoglycemia mimics stroke.

interventions.label: Lumbar puncture for CSF analysis if no contraindications and suspicion of meningitis or encephalitis
Consult Neurology, Infectious Disease, and/or Toxicology as needed
Oxygen
interventions.why: Immediate oxygen administration is crucial for any altered mental status case to maintain adequate oxygenation and prevent further deterioration.

interventions.label: IV access ×2
interventions.why: Establishing two large-bore IV access lines ensures the rapid delivery of fluids, medications, and blood products if needed.

easy.intro: Initial History Reason(s) for visit: Altered mental status History of present Illness: A 70-year-old Caucasian man comes to the Emergency room because his family members are concerned that something is wrong. The patient has been having behavioral changes over a period of three or four weeks. He used to be very active and managed all of his financial affairs. He now barely talks, stares at the wall for hours at a time, and sleeps most of the day. Four weeks ago, the patient got up to urinate and fell before reaching the bathroom. He was found on the floor the next morning by family members. He was taken to his family doctor and was discharged with a prescription to buy a cane. Since that time, he has been having problems walking. There have been no fevers or night sweats. The patient is currently in no pain. Past Medical History: Chronic alcohol abuse; cataracts. Hospitalization Procedures: Hemorrhoidectomy 1 year ago. Last colonoscopy was 4 years ago which demonstrated no abnormalities. Current medications: Aspirin 81mg PO daily Allergies: None Vaccinations: Up to date Family History: Father: Deceased due to a 'heart attack'. Mother: Diagnosed with type 2 diabetes mellitus and died of natural causes. The patient has no siblings. Social History: Marital/Family: Widowed; he has three children. Personal habits: the patient smokes 1 Pack/day. He currently does not drink alcohol but was a chronic alcoholic for 30 years. Denies use of illegal drugs or misuse of medications. Occupation/educational: He is a retired Army human resources manager. He is a high school graduate. Recreational: The patient likes to spend time with his children and enjoys collecting stamps. easy.intro: Initial History Reason(s) for visit: Altered mental status History of present Illness: A 70-year-old Caucasian man comes to the Emergency room because his family members are concerned that something is wrong. I has been having behavioral changes over a period of three or four weeks. I used to be very active and managed all of his financial affairs. I now barely talks, stares at the wall for hours at a time, and sleeps most of the day. Four weeks ago, I got up to urinate and fell before reaching the bathroom. I was found on the floor the next morning by family members. I was taken to his family doctor and was discharged with a prescription to buy a cane. 094.title: Rash with Joint Pain
094.diagnosis: Possible Autoimmune Disorder or Drug Reaction
exam: Rash with Joint Pain — exam tailored to chief complaint on arrival.
Soft, non-distended, no focal peritoneal signs on initial exam.
Alert and oriented unless perfusion or metabolic derangement present.
Skin rash morphology and distribution documented.
094.clinical_tip: Petechiae + fever in a sick patient — treat empirically for meningococcemia.
094.objective: Recognize toxic rash syndromes. Resuscitate and cover infection.

interventions.label: Laboratory tests: Complete blood count (CBC), Comprehensive metabolic panel, Erythrocyte sedimentation rate (ESR), C-reactive protein (CRP), Antinuclear antibody (ANA), Rheumatoid factor, Liver function tests (LFTs)
Imaging studies: Consider joint imaging (X-ray, MRI) if localized joint pain or swelling
Dermatology consultation for skin lesion evaluation and biopsy

095.title: Vaginal Bleeding
095.diagnosis: Vaginal bleeding could be due to various causes such as hormonal imbalances, infections, trauma, or neoplastic conditions. However, it's crucial to note that the given scenario is a USMLE Step 3 CCS (Clinical Case Simulation) with an unknown diagnosis. In this context, the focus should be on initial stabilization and diagnostic steps rather than assuming a specific etiology.
exam: Vaginal Bleeding — exam tailored to chief complaint on arrival.
Soft, non-distended, no focal peritoneal signs on initial exam.
Alert and oriented unless perfusion or metabolic derangement present.
No acute rash; capillary refill and perfusion assessed.
095.clinical_tip: Positive pregnancy test changes everything — ectopic until proven otherwise.
095.objective: Rule out ectopic, torsion, and rupture.

interventions.label: Laboratory Tests: Complete Blood Count (CBC), Coagulation Panel, Pregnancy Test, HIV Test, Hepatitis B and C Serologies, Syphilis RPR/VDRL
Consults: Obstetrics & Gynecology if the patient is of childbearing age or has recent sexual activity
β-hCG
interventions.why: Vaginal bleeding in a reproductive age woman requires immediate pregnancy testing.

096.title: Nausea and Vomiting
096.diagnosis: Acute gastroenteritis or possibly other gastrointestinal disorders based on the presentation of nausea and vomiting
exam: Nausea and Vomiting — exam tailored to chief complaint on arrival.
Abdominal tenderness pattern matches history; no rigid abdomen documented yet.
Alert and oriented unless perfusion or metabolic derangement present.
No acute rash; capillary refill and perfusion assessed.
096.clinical_tip: Elderly and immunocompromised may have minimal exam findings until rupture.
096.objective: Resuscitate, image, involve surgery early if indicated.

interventions.label: Obtain laboratory tests: CBC, electrolytes, amylase, lipase
Obtain imaging studies: Abdominal X-ray or CT scan if symptoms persist or complications suspected
Oxygen
interventions.why: Nausea and Vomiting can be signs of various life-threatening conditions such as sepsis, gastrointestinal bleed, or metabolic disorders. Immediate oxygen administration is crucial to prevent hypoxia.

interventions.label: IV access ×2

097.title: Dyspareunia
097.diagnosis: Vulvodynia or Vestibulodynia (common causes of dyspareunia in women)
exam: Dyspareunia — exam tailored to chief complaint on arrival.

No acute rash; capillary refill and perfusion assessed.
097.clinical_tip: Positive pregnancy test changes everything — ectopic until proven otherwise.

interventions.label: Obtain a detailed history, including a gynecologic and sexual history
Perform a physical exam focusing on the vulva, cervix, and vagina
Consider referral to a gynecologist or pelvic pain specialist for further evaluation
Order laboratory tests (e.g., complete blood count, thyroid function tests) if other potential causes are suspected
Prescribe analgesics for symptomatic relief and recommend pelvic floor physical therapy as needed
β-hCG
interventions.why: Dyspareunia can be related to various reproductive issues, and a pregnancy test is essential in women of childbearing age.

098.title: Back Pain
098.diagnosis: Acute Low Back Strain or Strain/Strain (Whiplash Injury) is the most likely diagnosis given the presented chief complaint of back pain with an unknown cause.
exam: Back Pain — exam tailored to chief complaint on arrival.
Soft, non-distended, no focal peritoneal signs on initial exam.
Alert and oriented unless perfusion or metabolic derangement present.
No acute rash; capillary refill and perfusion assessed.
098.clinical_tip: Weakness + hyperreflexia vs hyporeflexia localizes — think electrolytes and cord.
098.objective: Differentiate metabolic, neurologic, and toxic causes.

interventions.label: Obtain a detailed history, including mechanism of injury, onset and progression of symptoms, associated symptoms (e.g., radicular symptoms, neurological deficits), and any aggravating or alleviating factors

Physical exam focusing on the lumbar spine, muscle strength testing, reflexes, sensory examination, and special tests such as straight leg raise and Neurological Level Sign (NLS) to assess for radiculopathy
Oxygen
interventions.why: To provide adequate oxygenation in case of respiratory distress

interventions.label: IV access ×2
interventions.why: For rapid administration of fluids and medications if needed

interventions.label: Glucose check
interventions.why: To quickly assess blood glucose level, as low or high levels can cause symptoms similar to back pain
